cell-biology/eukaryotes-prokaryotes.summary: Compare the key features of eukaryotic and prokaryotic cells, including scale and size.

quiz.q: What is the key structural difference between eukaryotic and prokaryotic cells?
opts: Eukaryotes have a membrane-bound nucleus; prokaryotes do not
Prokaryotes are larger and more complex than eukaryotes
Eukaryotes have no cell membrane
Prokaryotes have mitochondria but eukaryotes do not

wrong_explanations.1: Prokaryotes are SMALLER and SIMPLER — roughly 10× smaller than a typical animal cell.
wrong_explanations.2: ALL cells have a cell membrane — it is the universal feature of all living cells.
wrong_explanations.3: It is the OPPOSITE — eukaryotes have mitochondria. Prokaryotes have NO membrane-bound organelles at all.

quiz.q: A bacterium is approximately 2 µm in length. How does this compare to a typical animal cell?
opts: The animal cell is roughly 10× larger — approximately 20 µm
They are about the same size
The bacterium is larger — bacteria are bigger than animal cells
The animal cell is 1000× larger than the bacterium

wrong_explanations.1: They are definitely not the same size — a bacterium at 2 µm vs an animal cell at ~20 µm is a clear 10× size difference.
wrong_explanations.2: Bacteria are SMALLER — roughly 10× smaller than animal cells, not larger.
wrong_explanations.3: 1000× larger would make the animal cell 2000 µm — about 2 mm! Animal cells are only ~10× larger, not 1000×.

quiz.q: Which of the following is found in a bacterial cell but NOT in a typical animal cell?
opts: Plasmids
Mitochondria
A nucleus
A cell membrane

wrong_explanations.1: Animal cells have mitochondria for aerobic respiration — bacteria do not have mitochondria.
wrong_explanations.2: Animal cells HAVE a nucleus — bacteria do not. The question asks what bacteria have that animal cells don't.
wrong_explanations.3: Both animal cells AND bacteria have a cell membrane — it is a universal feature.

quiz.q: 1 mm is equal to how many micrometres (µm)?
opts: 100 µm
10 µm

wrong_explanations.1: 100 µm = 0.1 mm. The conversion is 1 mm = 1000 µm.
wrong_explanations.2: 1,000,000 µm = 1 metre. The conversion you need is: 1 mm = 1000 µm.
wrong_explanations.3: 10 µm = 0.01 mm. Remember: milli means one thousandth, so 1 mm = 1000 µm.

quiz.q: What is a flagellum in a bacterial cell?
opts: A rotating tail-like structure used for movement
A small ring of extra DNA used for genetic transfer
A thick outer layer that protects the bacterium
The region of cytoplasm where the DNA is stored

wrong_explanations.1: A small ring of extra DNA = a PLASMID, not a flagellum.
wrong_explanations.2: A thick outer protective layer = the CAPSULE (or cell wall). The flagellum is the structure for movement.
wrong_explanations.3: The region where DNA sits loosely in the cytoplasm is called the nucleoid region — the flagellum is an external motor structure.

foundation.common_mistake: Prokaryotic cells have NO nucleus and NO membrane-bound organelles. Students often write 'bacteria have a nucleus' — they do not. The DNA is a single circular loop loose in the cytoplasm. Also: the bacterial cell wall is made of PEPTIDOGLYCAN, not cellulose (cellulose = plant cell walls).

fifas.question: A bacterium is drawn 30 mm long in a diagram. Its actual length is 0.003 mm. Calculate the magnification.
steps: Both values already in mm — no conversion needed

fifas.question: A cell is shown at ×500 magnification and measures 10 mm in the image. Calculate the actual size in µm.

cell-biology/animal-plant-cells.summary: Describe the sub-cellular structures of animal and plant cells and relate structure to function.

quiz.q: Which organelle is responsible for protein synthesis?
opts: Ribosomes
Mitochondria
Nucleus
Chloroplasts

wrong_explanations.1: Mitochondria are the site of aerobic RESPIRATION — they release energy from glucose, they don't make proteins.
wrong_explanations.2: The nucleus contains the DNA instructions for making proteins, but the actual assembly of amino acids into proteins happens at RIBOSOMES.
wrong_explanations.3: Chloroplasts carry out PHOTOSYNTHESIS — capturing light energy to make glucose. Not involved in protein synthesis.

quiz.q: A student looks at a plant cell from a root. They expect to see chloroplasts but find none. Why?
opts: Root cells are underground — no light reaches them, so chloroplasts are not present
Root cells are too small to contain chloroplasts
Root cells are prokaryotic and cannot have chloroplasts
Chloroplasts are only present in animal cells

wrong_explanations.1: Root cells are eukaryotic plant cells — size is not the reason. Palisade cells are similar in size and have many chloroplasts.
wrong_explanations.2: ALL plant cells are eukaryotic. Root cells are plant cells — they are eukaryotic. Only bacteria (and other prokaryotes) lack membrane-bound organelles.
wrong_explanations.3: Chloroplasts are found in PLANT cells — not animal cells. Animal cells never have chloroplasts.

quiz.q: What is the function of the permanent vacuole in a plant cell?
opts: Filled with cell sap — contributes to turgor pressure, keeping the cell firm
Stores chlorophyll for photosynthesis
Acts as the site of aerobic respiration
Controls what enters and leaves the cell

wrong_explanations.1: Chlorophyll is stored in CHLOROPLASTS — not the vacuole. The vacuole contains cell sap (sugars and salts).
wrong_explanations.2: Aerobic respiration occurs in MITOCHONDRIA. The vacuole is a storage compartment.
wrong_explanations.3: Controlling what enters and leaves = the CELL MEMBRANE. The vacuole is a storage structure filled with cell sap.

quiz.q: Which of the following is present in both animal and plant cells?
opts: Mitochondria
Cell wall
Chloroplasts
Permanent vacuole

wrong_explanations.1: Cell walls are only found in plant cells (and bacteria, fungi) — NOT in animal cells.
wrong_explanations.2: Chloroplasts are found only in plant cells that are exposed to light. Animal cells never have chloroplasts.
wrong_explanations.3: The permanent vacuole is a plant cell feature — animal cells do not have a large permanent vacuole (though they may have small temporary vacuoles).

quiz.q: Why do very active muscle cells contain many more mitochondria than skin cells?
opts: Muscle cells need much more ATP energy for contraction — more mitochondria produce more ATP
Muscle cells are larger, so they need more organelles to fill the space
Mitochondria help muscle cells to absorb oxygen from the blood
Skin cells don't respire — they don't need mitochondria at all

wrong_explanations.1: Cell size doesn't directly dictate organelle number — it is energy demand that determines mitochondria count.
wrong_explanations.2: Oxygen is absorbed by diffusion through the cell membrane — mitochondria USE oxygen in respiration, they don't absorb it.
wrong_explanations.3: All living cells respire all the time — skin cells do have mitochondria. Active cells simply have FAR MORE.

foundation.common_mistake: NOT all plant cells have chloroplasts — ONLY cells exposed to light. Root cells are underground so never receive light and have NO chloroplasts. Students also often forget that plant cells have a cell wall AND a cell membrane — both are present. The cell wall is outside, the cell membrane is inside it.

cell-biology/cell-specialisation.summary: Explain how and why cells become specialised, with examples from animals and plants.

quiz.q: Why does a sperm cell contain many mitochondria?
opts: It needs large amounts of ATP energy to power its flagellum and swim to the egg
Mitochondria help the sperm digest through the egg membrane
More mitochondria make the sperm cell larger and more visible
Mitochondria store the sperm's genetic material

wrong_explanations.1: Digesting the egg membrane is the function of the ACROSOME — an enzyme-filled cap at the tip of the sperm head. Mitochondria provide energy.
wrong_explanations.2: Sperm cells are actually very small and streamlined to reduce drag — larger would mean slower swimming. Mitochondria provide energy, not bulk.
wrong_explanations.3: Genetic material (DNA) is stored in the NUCLEUS, not in mitochondria. Mitochondria have their own small amount of DNA but this is not what carries genetic information to the egg.

quiz.q: A red blood cell has no nucleus. How does this benefit its function?
opts: More internal space for haemoglobin, allowing the cell to carry more oxygen
Without a nucleus it cannot be destroyed by white blood cells
It can reproduce more quickly without a nucleus getting in the way
The absence of a nucleus reduces the cell's oxygen needs

wrong_explanations.1: White blood cells do patrol for pathogens, but red blood cell lifespan (~120 days) is determined by membrane wear — not by whether they have a nucleus.
wrong_explanations.2: Cells without a nucleus CANNOT reproduce — red blood cells are made in bone marrow and released as mature, non-dividing cells.
wrong_explanations.3: Cells don't 'need' oxygen for the nucleus — they need it for respiration in mitochondria. Red blood cells actually have very few mitochondria and mainly use anaerobic respiration.

quiz.q: Why does a root hair cell have no chloroplasts?
opts: Root hair cells are underground — there is no light available for photosynthesis
Root hair cells are too small to fit chloroplasts inside
Chloroplasts would prevent water absorption from the soil
Root hair cells are a type of prokaryotic cell and cannot have organelles

wrong_explanations.1: Root hair cells are of similar size to leaf cells — size is not the limiting factor. The absence of light is the reason.
wrong_explanations.2: Chloroplasts don't block water entry — they are simply absent because they would serve no purpose underground where there is no light.
wrong_explanations.3: All plant cells are EUKARYOTIC. Root hair cells are eukaryotic plant cells — they could have organelles, but chloroplasts specifically are not present due to the lack of light.

quiz.q: What structural feature of xylem cells makes them well-suited for water transport?
opts: They are dead and hollow — no cell contents blocking the continuous water column
They contain many chloroplasts to produce energy for pumping water upwards
They have a large vacuole to store water temporarily
They have very thin walls to allow water to pass through easily

wrong_explanations.1: Chloroplasts would only be present if the xylem cell was exposed to light — and the purpose of xylem is water transport, not photosynthesis. The hollow dead cells are the key feature.
wrong_explanations.2: Xylem cells don't store water — they are a continuous flow pipe. Vacuoles are a feature of living plant cells for support.
wrong_explanations.3: Xylem walls are actually THICK and reinforced with lignin — this gives them strength to withstand the tension created as water is pulled upwards, preventing the xylem from collapsing.

foundation.common_mistake: Students often say 'root hair cells absorb water by active transport' — this is WRONG. Water enters by OSMOSIS (passive). Mineral IONS are absorbed by active transport. These are two different processes happening in the same cell. Don't mix them up.

cell-biology/microscopy.summary: Compare light and electron microscopes, and carry out magnification calculations.

quiz.q: What is the maximum magnification of a light microscope?
opts: Approximately ×2,000
Approximately ×2,000,000
Approximately ×200
Approximately ×20,000

wrong_explanations.1: ×2,000,000 is the maximum for an ELECTRON microscope — far more powerful than a light microscope.
wrong_explanations.2: ×200 is a common low-power objective on a school microscope — but the total maximum for light microscopes is around ×2,000.
wrong_explanations.3: ×20,000 doesn't correspond to either type — light microscopes reach ~×2,000 and electron microscopes ~×2,000,000.

quiz.q: An image is 45 mm wide. The actual size is 0.009 mm. What is the magnification?

wrong_explanations.2: 45 ÷ 0.009 = 5000 not 50,000. Check you moved the decimal correctly.
wrong_explanations.3: 45 minus 0.009 is not how you calculate magnification! Use M = I ÷ A = 45 ÷ 0.009 = 5000.

quiz.q: Why were ribosomes not discovered until electron microscopes became available?
opts: Ribosomes (~20 nm) are far below the resolution limit of light microscopes (~200 nm)
Ribosomes are only visible in living cells and electron microscopes can see living cells
Ribosomes are colourless and no stain existed until recently
Ribosomes only appear in plant cells which were studied less

wrong_explanations.1: Electron microscopes cannot view LIVING specimens — they require dead specimens in a vacuum. The key advantage is RESOLUTION, not viewing live cells.
wrong_explanations.2: Staining could make ribosomes coloured — but even stained, they are too small to be resolved by a light microscope.
wrong_explanations.3: Ribosomes are found in ALL cells — animal, plant and bacterial. They were not limited to plant cells.

quiz.q: What is the difference between magnification and resolution?
opts: Magnification = how much bigger the image appears; resolution = how clearly fine detail can be distinguished
They are the same thing — both refer to how large the image looks
Magnification refers to electron microscopes only; resolution applies to light microscopes
Resolution is measured in × and magnification is measured in nm

wrong_explanations.1: They are NOT the same. You can have a highly magnified but blurry image — that is high magnification but low resolution.
wrong_explanations.2: Both types of microscope have both magnification and resolution — the terms apply to all microscopes.
wrong_explanations.3: Magnification has no unit (×) — it is a ratio. Resolution is measured in nm (nanometres). You have these exactly the wrong way around.

quiz.q: A cell has an actual diameter of 50 µm. At ×400 magnification, how large will the image appear in µm?
opts: 450 µm

wrong_explanations.1: You divided instead of multiplying. Image = Actual × Magnification = 50 × 400 = 20,000 µm.
wrong_explanations.2: You added the magnification to the actual size. Image = Actual × Magnification = 50 × 400 = 20,000 µm.
wrong_explanations.3: You used the wrong magnification value. Image = 50 × 400 = 20,000 µm — not 2000 µm.

foundation.common_mistake: Units must be the same before calculating. If image size is in mm and actual size is in µm, you MUST convert first or your answer will be wrong by a factor of 1000. Always write out the units in your working — this forces you to spot when they don't match.

fifas.label: Example 1 — Find magnification
fifas.question: A cell appears 54 mm wide in a diagram. Its actual width is 0.018 mm. Calculate the magnification.
steps: Both values are in mm — no unit conversion needed

fifas.label: Example 2 — Find actual size
fifas.question: A mitochondrion appears 12 mm long in an electron micrograph at ×80,000 magnification. Calculate its actual length in µm.

fifas.label: Example 3 — Find image size
fifas.question: A bacterium has an actual diameter of 2 µm. It is drawn at ×2000 magnification. How large should the drawing be in mm?

higher.higher_box: Electron microscopes have much greater magnification and resolution than light microscopes — they can reveal sub-cellular structures invisible to light microscopes. Resolution is the ability to distinguish two points; light microscopes are limited by the wavelength of light (~200 nm). Scanning electron microscopes (SEM) show surface detail; transmission electron microscopes (TEM) show internal structure. Use the magnification formula: magnification = image size ÷ actual size. Convert between mm, μm and nm when calculating.

cell-biology/chromosomes-mitosis.summary: Describe chromosomes, the stages of the cell cycle and the process and purpose of mitosis.

quiz.q: A human body cell has 46 chromosomes. How many chromosomes will each daughter cell have after mitosis?

wrong_explanations.1: 23 chromosomes = the haploid number found in GAMETES after meiosis. Mitosis maintains the FULL chromosome number.
wrong_explanations.2: 92 is the number of chromatids DURING DNA replication — before the cell divides. After mitosis, each daughter cell has 46.
wrong_explanations.3: 12 has no biological relevance here. The answer is always the same as the parent cell — mitosis maintains chromosome number.

quiz.q: What must happen to DNA before a cell can divide by mitosis?
opts: It must be replicated — each chromosome is copied so the cell has double the DNA
The DNA must be destroyed and completely rebuilt from scratch
The chromosome number must be halved by meiosis first
Nothing — the DNA divides automatically with no prior preparation

wrong_explanations.1: DNA is never destroyed during the cell cycle — it is carefully replicated and preserved. Destroying it would kill the cell.
wrong_explanations.2: Halving chromosomes = MEIOSIS. Mitosis MAINTAINS chromosome number. You cannot prepare for mitosis by halving DNA.
wrong_explanations.3: DNA replication MUST occur before division. Each daughter cell needs a complete set of chromosomes — if DNA wasn't replicated, one cell would get half and the other cell would get the other half.

quiz.q: What is the difference between a benign and a malignant tumour?
opts: Benign stays in one place; malignant spreads to other parts of the body (metastasis)
Benign is caused by genetics; malignant is caused by lifestyle only
Malignant tumours are always fatal; benign tumours always disappear on their own
Benign tumours are larger than malignant tumours

wrong_explanations.1: Both benign and malignant tumours can have genetic causes and both can be influenced by lifestyle. The defining difference is whether the tumour SPREADS.
wrong_explanations.2: Malignant tumours vary hugely in outlook — many can be treated successfully. And benign tumours don't always disappear — they may need surgical removal.
wrong_explanations.3: Tumour size does not define whether it is benign or malignant. Malignant tumours are defined by their ability to invade and spread.

quiz.q: Which of the following best describes the purpose of mitosis?
opts: Growth, repair and replacement of body cells — producing genetically identical daughter cells
Producing gametes (sperm and eggs) with half the chromosome number
Creating genetic variation in the offspring of an organism
Repairing damaged DNA before it can be copied

wrong_explanations.1: Producing gametes = MEIOSIS. Gametes need half the chromosome number — mitosis would give them the full 46.
wrong_explanations.2: Mitosis produces IDENTICAL cells — there is no genetic variation. Genetic variation comes from meiosis and mutation.
wrong_explanations.3: DNA repair mechanisms exist but are separate from mitosis. Mitosis is the division process, not the repair process.

quiz.q: Cancer is caused by...
opts: Mutations in genes that control the cell cycle — causing uncontrolled cell division
Too many mitochondria in a cell causing overproduction of ATP
Cells reversing their differentiation and becoming stem cells again
The immune system attacking healthy body cells

wrong_explanations.1: Mitochondria number has nothing to do with cancer. Cancer is caused by loss of cell cycle regulation due to gene mutation.
wrong_explanations.2: Some cancer cells do share characteristics with stem cells (undifferentiation), but cancer is specifically caused by mutations in regulatory genes — not by a deliberate reversal of differentiation.
wrong_explanations.3: The immune system attacking healthy cells = autoimmune disease (e.g. Type 1 diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis). Cancer is caused by mutated regulatory genes.

Do NOT confuse with meiosis (not required at Foundation but you will hear the word). Meiosis produces FOUR cells each with HALF the chromosomes — used only for making gametes. Mitosis = for the BODY. Meiosis = for GAMETES.

quiz.q: A human body cell has 46 chromosomes. How many chromosomes will each daughter cell have after mitosis?

wrong_explanations.1: 23 chromosomes = the haploid number found in GAMETES after meiosis. Mitosis maintains the FULL chromosome number.
wrong_explanations.2: 92 is the number of chromatids DURING DNA replication — before the cell divides. After mitosis, each daughter cell has 46.
wrong_explanations.3: 12 has no biological relevance here. The answer is always the same as the parent cell — mitosis maintains chromosome number.

The DNA must be destroyed and completely rebuilt from scratch
The chromosome number must be halved by meiosis first
Nothing — the DNA divides automatically with no prior preparation

cell-biology/stem-cells.summary: Describe the properties and uses of stem cells, including embryonic, adult and plant meristem cells.

quiz.q: What does 'totipotent' mean when describing stem cells?
opts: The cell can differentiate into any type of cell in the organism
The cell can only differentiate into cells of one specific tissue type
The cell can divide indefinitely without ever differentiating
The cell has already partially specialised and cannot reverse

wrong_explanations.1: Differentiating into cells of one tissue type = MULTIPOTENT (e.g. bone marrow stem cells → blood cells only). Totipotent = ANY cell type.
wrong_explanations.2: The ability to divide indefinitely is SELF-RENEWAL — a separate property of stem cells. Totipotency refers to differentiation potential.
wrong_explanations.3: Partially specialised cells that cannot reverse are already differentiated cells — not stem cells at all.

quiz.q: How are stem cells used to treat leukaemia?
opts: A bone marrow transplant: cancerous blood cells destroyed, then donor stem cells replace them and produce healthy blood cells
Embryonic stem cells are injected into the blood to replace cancer cells
Stem cells are used to make antibodies that kill cancer cells directly
Stem cells repair the damaged DNA in leukaemia cells, reversing the cancer

wrong_explanations.1: Embryonic stem cells are not used for leukaemia treatment — adult bone marrow stem cells are used. They are donated or sometimes the patient's own (after treatment).
wrong_explanations.2: Antibodies that target cancer cells are a different therapy (monoclonal antibodies) — not how stem cells work in leukaemia treatment.
wrong_explanations.3: Stem cells don't repair cancer cell DNA — the cancer cells are destroyed by chemotherapy/radiotherapy first, then NEW healthy cells are produced from transplanted stem cells.

quiz.q: Why are embryonic stem cells ethically controversial?
opts: Obtaining them requires destroying an embryo — which some consider to be destroying a potential human life
They cause cancer in patients who receive them
They are ineffective at treating disease
They are too expensive to collect and store

wrong_explanations.1: Cancer risk is a technical concern — the main ETHICAL controversy is about the moral status of the embryo that is destroyed.
wrong_explanations.2: Embryonic stem cells are actually MORE effective than adult stem cells because they are totipotent — the concern is ethical, not about effectiveness.
wrong_explanations.3: Cost and practicality are challenges — but the defining controversy is ethical: is it acceptable to destroy a potential human life for medical research?

quiz.q: What is special about plant meristem cells compared to adult animal stem cells?
opts: Plant meristem cells are totipotent — they can form any plant cell type; adult animal stem cells are only multipotent
Plant meristem cells can only form root cells; animal stem cells are more versatile
Plant meristem cells are found throughout all plant tissues; animal stem cells are only in bone marrow
There is no difference — both types have the same level of potency

wrong_explanations.1: It is almost the opposite — plant meristems are MORE versatile (totipotent), not less.
wrong_explanations.2: Meristems are found in specific regions (root tips, shoot tips, cambium) — not throughout all tissues. And they can form any plant cell type, not just root cells.
wrong_explanations.3: There is a significant difference in potency — plant meristem cells are essentially totipotent while most adult animal stem cells are multipotent.

foundation.common_mistake: Students often say stem cells 'replace organs' — they don't. They differentiate into SPECIFIC CELL TYPES that can be used to repair or replace damaged cells within an organ. Also: embryonic stem cells are TOTIPOTENT (any cell type). Adult stem cells are MULTIPOTENT (limited range). Don't mix these terms up.

wrong_explanations.3: There is a significant difference in potency — plant meristem cells are essentially totipotent while most adult animal stem cells are multipotent.

Also: embryonic stem cells are TOTIPOTENT (any cell type). Adult stem cells are MULTIPOTENT (limited range). Don't mix these terms up.

Stem cells from this embryo will not be rejected by the patient's immune system. Risks include viral transfer. Ethical issues: some object to embryo use. Plant meristems provide a source of totipotent stem cells for rapid cloning of rare or disease-resistant varieties.

cell-biology/transport-in-cells.summary: Describe and compare the three ways substances move into and out of cells.

quiz.q: Which process requires ATP energy to move substances across a cell membrane?
opts: Active transport
Diffusion
Osmosis
Both diffusion and osmosis

wrong_explanations.1: Diffusion is PASSIVE — particles move due to their own random kinetic energy, not ATP.
wrong_explanations.2: Osmosis is also PASSIVE — water moves down its own concentration gradient without energy input.
wrong_explanations.3: NEITHER diffusion nor osmosis requires ATP. Only active transport requires energy — that is what distinguishes it from the other two.

quiz.q: A potato cylinder is placed in pure water. What happens and why?
opts: It gains mass — water enters by osmosis from the dilute water into the more concentrated cell contents
It loses mass — salt leaves the potato into the water by diffusion
Nothing — the potato is not permeable to water
It gains mass because water enters by active transport

wrong_explanations.1: Salt does diffuse slightly — but the dominant effect is water moving by OSMOSIS into the cells, increasing mass.
wrong_explanations.2: Cell membranes are selectively permeable — water moves freely across them by osmosis. The potato is very much permeable to water.
wrong_explanations.3: Osmosis is PASSIVE — it requires no energy input. Water moves down its concentration gradient automatically.

quiz.q: Why does active transport stop if oxygen is removed from the environment of a cell?
opts: No oxygen → aerobic respiration stops → no ATP produced → no energy for active transport
Oxygen is a carrier protein that directly powers active transport
Without oxygen, the cell membrane becomes impermeable to all substances
Removing oxygen changes the concentration gradient, making active transport unnecessary

wrong_explanations.1: Oxygen doesn't directly power carrier proteins — it is needed for aerobic respiration to produce ATP, which then powers the carriers.
wrong_explanations.2: Membranes remain structurally intact without oxygen — it is the ATP supply that is lost, not membrane structure.
wrong_explanations.3: Removing oxygen doesn't change concentration gradients. It removes the ATP supply needed to pump substances against those gradients.

quiz.q: A plant cell is placed in a very concentrated salt solution. What will happen?
opts: Water leaves by osmosis → the cell becomes flaccid → the cell membrane pulls away from the cell wall (plasmolysis)
Water enters by osmosis → the cell becomes turgid and very firm
Salt enters by active transport → the cell becomes denser
The cell bursts because it cannot handle the osmotic pressure

wrong_explanations.1: Water enters when the EXTERNAL solution is MORE DILUTE than the cell contents. In concentrated salt solution, it is the other way round — water leaves.
wrong_explanations.2: Salt ions may slowly diffuse in, but the dominant effect is OSMOSIS — water moves to the MORE CONCENTRATED solution (the salt solution outside), not into the cell.
wrong_explanations.3: Plant cells have a rigid CELL WALL that prevents bursting — unlike animal cells. A plant cell can become flaccid or plasmolyse, but it won't burst.

quiz.q: What is the definition of osmosis?
opts: The net movement of water molecules across a partially permeable membrane from a dilute solution to a more concentrated solution
The movement of any particles from high to low concentration — passive
The movement of water molecules against a concentration gradient using ATP
The diffusion of water molecules through any membrane in any direction

wrong_explanations.1: The movement of ANY particles from high to low = DIFFUSION. Osmosis is specifically about WATER ONLY through a PARTIALLY PERMEABLE membrane.
wrong_explanations.2: Movement against a gradient using ATP = ACTIVE TRANSPORT. Osmosis is passive — water moves DOWN its concentration gradient.
wrong_explanations.3: Osmosis only occurs through a PARTIALLY PERMEABLE membrane, not any membrane. And it only moves water in ONE net direction — from dilute to concentrated.

foundation.common_mistake: Osmosis involves WATER ONLY — through a PARTIALLY PERMEABLE MEMBRANE. Glucose, ions and other molecules do NOT move by osmosis. Active transport goes AGAINST the gradient and requires ATP energy — without ATP (no respiration) it stops. Diffusion and osmosis are both PASSIVE — no ATP needed. Never say 'diffusion requires energy'.

fifas.label: Osmosis — Percentage Change in Mass
fifas.question: A potato cylinder has a mass of 4.0 g before being placed in a concentrated sugar solution. After 30 minutes it has a mass of 3.4 g. Calculate the percentage change in mass.
steps: % change = (final mass − initial mass) ÷ initial mass × 100
% change = −15% (a decrease — water left by osmosis into the concentrated solution)

The isotonic point — where a plant tissue neither gains nor loses mass — is where the external solution concentration matches the cell's water potential. Exchange surfaces are adapted with large surface area, thin walls, good blood supply/ventilation. Students should be able to explain and calculate surface area to volume ratios and explain why large organisms need exchange systems.

organisation/principles-of-organisation.summary: Describe the levels of organisation in living organisms from cells to organisms.

quiz.q: What is the correct order of organisation from simplest to most complex?
opts: Cell → Tissue → Organ → Organ system → Organism
Tissue → Cell → Organ → Organ system → Organism
Cell → Organ → Tissue → Organism → Organ system
Organism → Organ system → Organ → Tissue → Cell

wrong_explanations.1: Tissues are made of cells — cells always come before tissues in the hierarchy.
wrong_explanations.2: Organs contain tissues, not the other way round — tissue must come before organ.
wrong_explanations.3: This is the correct order in reverse — from most complex down to simplest.

quiz.q: What is a tissue?
opts: A group of similar cells working together to perform a specific function
A group of different organs working together
A single highly specialised cell
Another name for an organ

wrong_explanations.1: A group of different organs = an ORGAN SYSTEM. Tissues are made of similar cells, not organs.
wrong_explanations.2: A single specialised cell is just a cell — not a tissue. A tissue is a GROUP of cells.
wrong_explanations.3: Tissues and organs are different levels. An organ is made of SEVERAL tissue types.

quiz.q: The stomach contains muscle tissue, glandular tissue and epithelial tissue. What level of organisation is the stomach?
opts: Organ
Tissue
Organ system
Organism

wrong_explanations.1: A tissue is made of ONE type of cell — the stomach has multiple tissue types, so it is an organ.
wrong_explanations.2: An organ system is a GROUP of organs — the stomach is just one organ within the digestive system.
wrong_explanations.3: An organism is the complete living thing — a human, plant or bacterium.

quiz.q: Which of these is an example of an organ system?
opts: The digestive system — stomach, intestines, liver and pancreas working together
Muscle tissue — cells that contract to produce movement
The heart — contains cardiac muscle and valves
A liver cell — specialised to carry out chemical reactions

wrong_explanations.1: Muscle tissue = a TISSUE (one cell type). Organ systems are made of multiple organs.
wrong_explanations.2: The heart = an ORGAN (contains multiple tissue types). An organ system requires multiple organs.
wrong_explanations.3: A liver cell = a CELL — the simplest level of organisation.

foundation.common_mistake: Students confuse tissue and organ. Remember: a tissue is ONE type of cell doing ONE job. An organ contains MULTIPLE tissue types. The stomach is an ORGAN (it has muscle tissue, glandular tissue and epithelial tissue). Muscle is a TISSUE.

organisation/digestive-system.summary: Describe the organs of the digestive system and what happens to food at each stage.

quiz.q: What is the role of bile in digestion?
opts: Emulsifies fats — breaks large droplets into smaller ones to increase surface area for lipase
Chemically digests fats into fatty acids and glycerol
Kills bacteria in the small intestine using its acidity
Digests proteins into amino acids in the small intestine

wrong_explanations.1: Chemical digestion of fats = LIPASE. Bile only emulsifies — a physical process.
wrong_explanations.2: Bile is ALKALINE, not acidic. HCl in the stomach kills bacteria. Bile actually neutralises the stomach acid.
wrong_explanations.3: Protein digestion = PROTEASES (pepsin in the stomach, pancreatic proteases in the small intestine). Bile is not a protease.

quiz.q: Why does the small intestine have villi?
opts: To provide a large surface area for absorbing digested nutrients into the blood
To produce digestive enzymes for breaking down food
To neutralise the acid from the stomach
To store bile before it is released onto food

wrong_explanations.1: Digestive enzymes in the small intestine come from the PANCREAS — the villi are for absorption, not enzyme production.
wrong_explanations.2: Bile neutralises stomach acid — villi are surface-area adaptations for absorption.
wrong_explanations.3: Bile is stored in the GALL BLADDER, not in villi.

quiz.q: Where are proteins first chemically digested?
opts: The stomach — by the enzyme pepsin in the presence of HCl
The mouth — by salivary amylase
The large intestine — by bacteria
The liver — by bile salts

wrong_explanations.1: Salivary amylase in the mouth digests STARCH, not proteins. Amylase has no effect on protein.
wrong_explanations.2: Bacteria in the large intestine help break down some fibre but are not the main site of protein digestion.
wrong_explanations.3: The liver produces bile which emulsifies FATS — bile has no protein-digesting function.

quiz.q: What does the large intestine absorb?
opts: Water — from the remaining undigested material
Glucose — the final stage of carbohydrate absorption
Amino acids — the final stage of protein absorption
Fatty acids — the final stage of fat absorption

wrong_explanations.1: Glucose is absorbed in the SMALL INTESTINE — by the time food reaches the large intestine, glucose has already been absorbed.
wrong_explanations.2: Amino acids are absorbed in the SMALL INTESTINE — through the villi walls into the blood.
wrong_explanations.3: Fatty acids and glycerol are absorbed in the SMALL INTESTINE into the lacteals (lymph vessels in villi).

quiz.q: Which enzyme is produced by the salivary glands?
opts: Amylase — breaks down starch into maltose
Pepsin — breaks down proteins
Lipase — breaks down fats
Bile — emulsifies fats

wrong_explanations.1: Pepsin is produced by glandular tissue in the STOMACH WALL — not the salivary glands.
wrong_explanations.2: Lipase is produced by the PANCREAS — not the salivary glands.
wrong_explanations.3: Bile is not an enzyme — it is produced by the LIVER and stored in the gall bladder.

foundation.common_mistake: Bile is NOT an enzyme and does NOT chemically digest fats. It EMULSIFIES them — a physical process that breaks large fat droplets into smaller ones to give lipase more surface area to work on. Students often say 'bile digests fat' — it does not. LIPASE chemically digests fat.

quiz.q: What is the role of bile in digestion?
opts: Emulsifies fats — breaks large droplets into smaller ones to increase surface area for lipase
Chemically digests fats into fatty acids and glycerol
Kills bacteria in the small intestine using its acidity
Digests proteins into amino acids in the small intestine

wrong_explanations.1: Chemical digestion of fats = LIPASE. Bile only emulsifies — a physical process.
wrong_explanations.2: Bile is ALKALINE, not acidic. HCl in the stomach kills bacteria. Bile actually neutralises the stomach acid.
wrong_explanations.3: Protein digestion = PROTEASES (pepsin in the stomach, pancreatic proteases in the small intestine). Bile is not a protease.

quiz.q: Why does the small intestine have villi?
opts: To provide a large surface area for absorbing digested nutrients into the blood
To produce digestive enzymes for breaking down food
To neutralise the acid from the stomach
To store bile before it is released onto food

wrong_explanations.1: Digestive enzymes in the small intestine come from the PANCREAS — the villi are for absorption, not enzyme production.
wrong_explanations.2: Bile neutralises stomach acid — villi are surface-area adaptations for absorption.
wrong_explanations.3: Bile is stored in the GALL BLADDER, not in villi.

quiz.q: Where are proteins first chemically digested?
opts: The stomach — by the enzyme pepsin in the presence of HCl
The mouth — by salivary amylase
The large intestine — by bacteria
The liver — by bile salts

wrong_explanations.1: Salivary amylase in the mouth digests STARCH, not proteins. Amylase has no effect on protein.
wrong_explanations.2: Bacteria in the large intestine help break down some fibre but are not the main site of protein digestion.
wrong_explanations.3: The liver produces bile which emulsifies FATS — bile has no protein-digesting function.

quiz.q: What does the large intestine absorb?
opts: Water — from the remaining undigested material
Glucose — the final stage of carbohydrate absorption
Amino acids — the final stage of protein absorption
Fatty acids — the final stage of fat absorption

wrong_explanations.1: Glucose is absorbed in the SMALL INTESTINE — by the time food reaches the large intestine, glucose has already been absorbed.
wrong_explanations.2: Amino acids are absorbed in the SMALL INTESTINE — through the villi walls into the blood.
wrong_explanations.3: Fatty acids and glycerol are absorbed in the SMALL INTESTINE into the lacteals (lymph vessels in villi).

Pepsin — breaks down proteins
Lipase — breaks down fats
Bile — emulsifies fats

wrong_explanations.1: Pepsin is produced by glandular tissue in the STOMACH WALL — not the salivary glands.
wrong_explanations.2: Lipase is produced by the PANCREAS — not the salivary glands.
wrong_explanations.3: Bile is not an enzyme — it is produced by the LIVER and stored in the gall bladder.

higher.common_mistake: Bile is NOT an enzyme and does NOT chemically digest fats. It EMULSIFIES them — a physical process that breaks large fat droplets into smaller ones to give lipase more surface area to work on. Students often say 'bile digests fat' — it does not. LIPASE chemically digests fat.
higher.higher_box: Bile emulsifies fats — breaking large droplets into smaller ones, greatly increasing the surface area for lipase. The alkaline conditions created by bile neutralising stomach acid provide the optimum pH for pancreatic enzymes. Villi and microvilli on the intestinal lining further maximise surface area for absorption. Students should be able to explain how the small intestine is adapted for efficient absorption (large surface area, thin walls, rich blood supply).

organisation/enzymes.summary: Explain how enzymes work, how temperature and pH affect them, and describe the lock and key model.

quiz.q: An enzyme is heated to 80°C. What happens to it?
opts: It denatures — the active site shape permanently changes and the substrate can no longer bind
It works faster because higher temperature gives more energy
It is killed by the heat
It becomes temporarily inactive but recovers when cooled

wrong_explanations.1: Above the optimum (~37°C for human enzymes), the rate does increase — but 80°C is far above optimum, causing denaturation, not faster activity.
wrong_explanations.2: Enzymes are proteins, not living organisms — they cannot be 'killed'. Denaturation is the correct term.
wrong_explanations.3: Denaturation is PERMANENT. Once the active site shape is changed, cooling does NOT restore it.

quiz.q: Why does pepsin work best at pH 2?
opts: Pepsin works in the stomach where HCl creates pH 2 — the active site shape is optimal at this pH
All enzymes work best at pH 2 — it is the universal optimum
pH 2 provides more substrate molecules for pepsin to work on
Pepsin denatures at any pH above 2

wrong_explanations.1: Different enzymes have different optimum pH values. Salivary amylase works best at pH 7, pancreatic enzymes at pH 7–8. Only pepsin has an optimum around pH 2.
wrong_explanations.2: pH does not affect the number of substrate molecules — it affects the SHAPE of the active site.
wrong_explanations.3: Pepsin can work at a range of acidic pH values — though its optimum is around pH 2. It doesn't instantly denature above pH 2.

quiz.q: According to the lock and key model, why can amylase only digest starch — not proteins?
opts: Amylase's active site is shaped to fit starch molecules only — proteins have a different shape and cannot bind
Amylase is only produced in the mouth, where no proteins are found
Amylase is too small to interact with large protein molecules
Proteins repel enzymes and cannot form enzyme-substrate complexes

wrong_explanations.1: Amylase is produced in the mouth AND pancreas — it encounters proteins, but its active site shape doesn't match protein structure.
wrong_explanations.2: Enzyme size is not the issue — it's the shape of the ACTIVE SITE that determines which substrate can bind.
wrong_explanations.3: Proteins don't repel enzymes — proteases bind to proteins very effectively. It's specifically amylase whose active site doesn't fit proteins.

quiz.q: What happens to the rate of an enzyme-catalysed reaction when substrate concentration increases?
opts: Rate increases — more substrate molecules collide with active sites — until all active sites are occupied
Rate stays the same — substrate concentration does not affect enzyme activity
Rate decreases — more substrate molecules compete for fewer active sites
Rate immediately reaches maximum as soon as any substrate is added

wrong_explanations.1: Substrate concentration absolutely affects rate — more substrate = more collisions = faster rate (up to a limit).
wrong_explanations.2: Substrates don't 'compete' — each active site accepts substrate molecules one at a time. More substrate means MORE active sites occupied at any given moment.
wrong_explanations.3: Rate only reaches maximum when ALL active sites are occupied — the enzyme is then saturated. At low substrate concentrations, many active sites are empty.

quiz.q: After an enzyme-catalysed reaction, what happens to the enzyme?
opts: It is released unchanged and can catalyse another reaction — it is not used up
It is permanently bonded to the product and cannot be reused
It is broken down and must be rebuilt before working again
It loses its active site after each reaction and must reform it

wrong_explanations.1: Enzymes form temporary enzyme-SUBSTRATE complexes — once products are released, the enzyme is free and fully intact.
wrong_explanations.2: Enzymes are not broken down during catalysis — that's what makes them catalysts. They are reused many times.
wrong_explanations.3: Active sites are a permanent part of the enzyme's structure — they reform automatically because the enzyme is unchanged.

foundation.common_mistake: Enzymes are DENATURED at high temperatures — NOT killed. They are proteins, not living things. You cannot kill a protein. Denaturation means the active site shape is permanently changed. Cooling the enzyme down afterwards will NOT bring it back to life — the damage is permanent.

organisation/heart-blood-vessels.summary: Describe the structure and function of the heart, the double circulatory system and the three types of blood vessel.

quiz.q: Why does the left ventricle have thicker walls than the right ventricle?
opts: It pumps blood to the whole body — needs much higher pressure than the right ventricle which only pumps to the nearby lungs
It receives more blood than the right ventricle so needs to be stronger
The left side of the body is larger so the heart must compensate
The left ventricle contains oxygenated blood which is heavier

wrong_explanations.1: Both ventricles receive the same volume of blood per beat — the difference is in the distance the blood must be pumped, not the volume.
wrong_explanations.2: Body size has no relationship to heart wall thickness — it's purely about pumping distance and pressure.
wrong_explanations.3: Oxygenated and deoxygenated blood have essentially the same density — the thickness difference is purely about pressure requirements.

quiz.q: The pulmonary artery carries blood from the heart to the lungs. What type of blood does it carry?
opts: Deoxygenated blood — it has just returned from the body and needs to be reoxygenated
Oxygenated blood — all arteries carry oxygenated blood
A mixture of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood
Blood with no red blood cells — only plasma

wrong_explanations.1: This is the most common mistake in this topic! Arteries carry blood AWAY from the heart — but the pulmonary artery specifically carries deoxygenated blood to be oxygenated in the lungs.
wrong_explanations.2: Blood in the heart is kept completely separate — left side carries oxygenated, right side carries deoxygenated. There is no mixing.
wrong_explanations.3: All blood contains red blood cells — plasma alone would have no oxygen-carrying capacity.

quiz.q: Why do veins have valves but arteries do not?
opts: Blood pressure in veins is low — valves prevent backflow. Arterial pressure is high enough to keep blood flowing in the right direction
Arteries have thicker walls so they don't need valves
Veins carry deoxygenated blood which needs extra support to flow
Valves in arteries would slow down the high-pressure blood flow too much

wrong_explanations.1: Wall thickness helps arteries withstand pressure — but valves are specifically about preventing BACKFLOW, which is a problem in low-pressure veins, not high-pressure arteries.
wrong_explanations.2: The oxygen content of blood has nothing to do with the need for valves — the PRESSURE is what matters.
wrong_explanations.3: Valves in veins do slightly slow flow, but that's acceptable — their role is to prevent dangerous backflow in low-pressure vessels.

quiz.q: What makes capillaries efficient at exchanging substances?
opts: Walls are only one cell thick — very short diffusion distance. Dense network = large surface area
They have the highest blood pressure of all blood vessels
They carry both oxygenated and deoxygenated blood simultaneously
They have valves to keep substances moving in one direction

wrong_explanations.1: Capillaries have the LOWEST blood pressure — that is why they can be one cell thick without bursting.
wrong_explanations.2: Capillaries carry either oxygenated or deoxygenated blood depending on location — not both at once.
wrong_explanations.3: Capillaries have no valves — substances move by DIFFUSION driven by concentration gradients.

foundation.common_mistake: Arteries do NOT always carry oxygenated blood. The PULMONARY ARTERY carries DEOXYGENATED blood from the heart to the lungs. Veins do NOT always carry deoxygenated blood — the PULMONARY VEIN carries OXYGENATED blood from the lungs to the heart. The rule is: Arteries = Away from heart. Veins = to heart. Not about oxygen content.

Artificial hearts maintain circulation while waiting for a transplant or during recovery. Students should be able to evaluate the advantages and disadvantages of drug treatment vs mechanical devices vs transplant for cardiovascular disease.

organisation/blood.summary: Describe the four components of blood and the function of each.

quiz.q: Why do red blood cells have no nucleus?
opts: To create more space for haemoglobin — allowing the cell to carry more oxygen
So they can divide and replace themselves when worn out
To reduce their weight so they flow faster through blood vessels
So they can squeeze through capillaries more easily

wrong_explanations.1: Cells without a nucleus CANNOT divide — red blood cells are produced in bone marrow and cannot reproduce themselves.
wrong_explanations.2: Weight difference between a nucleus and haemoglobin molecules is negligible — the primary reason is space for haemoglobin.
wrong_explanations.3: Flexibility helps red blood cells squeeze through capillaries — but this is a result of their biconcave shape and flexibility, not the absence of a nucleus.

quiz.q: A patient has very few lymphocytes. What is the most likely consequence?
opts: Unable to produce specific antibodies — prolonged or repeated infections that are hard to clear
Unable to carry oxygen — severe anaemia
Blood will not clot properly — excessive bleeding from wounds
Unable to digest food properly — nutrients not absorbed

wrong_explanations.1: Oxygen carrying = RED BLOOD CELLS using haemoglobin. Lymphocytes are white blood cells.
wrong_explanations.2: Blood clotting = PLATELETS forming a fibrin mesh. Lymphocytes produce antibodies, not clotting factors.
wrong_explanations.3: Food digestion = digestive enzymes in the gut. Lymphocytes are part of the immune system.

quiz.q: What is the role of platelets in the body?
opts: Blood clotting — they clump at wound sites and trigger fibrin mesh formation to seal the damage
Carrying oxygen from the lungs to body tissues
Producing antibodies to fight specific pathogens
Engulfing and digesting bacteria by phagocytosis

wrong_explanations.1: Oxygen transport = RED BLOOD CELLS using haemoglobin.
wrong_explanations.2: Producing antibodies = LYMPHOCYTES (a type of white blood cell).
wrong_explanations.3: Engulfing bacteria = PHAGOCYTES (a type of white blood cell).

quiz.q: Which substance does plasma NOT transport?
opts: Oxygen — this is carried by haemoglobin inside red blood cells
Glucose from the small intestine to body cells
Urea from the liver to the kidneys
Carbon dioxide from respiring cells to the lungs

wrong_explanations.1: Glucose IS carried in plasma — after absorption from the small intestine.
wrong_explanations.2: Urea IS carried in plasma — from the liver to the kidneys for excretion.
wrong_explanations.3: Carbon dioxide IS dissolved in plasma — most CO₂ travels as bicarbonate ions dissolved in plasma.

foundation.common_mistake: Red blood cells carry oxygen — white blood cells do NOT carry oxygen. White blood cells fight infection. Platelets are NOT cells — they are cell fragments with no nucleus. They are involved in clotting, NOT in carrying oxygen or fighting infection.

organisation/coronary-heart-disease.summary: Describe the causes and treatments of coronary heart disease.

quiz.q: What causes coronary heart disease?
opts: Fatty plaques (atherosclerosis) build up in the coronary arteries, narrowing them and reducing blood flow to the heart muscle
The heart muscle becomes too weak to pump blood effectively due to overuse
Blood becomes too thick and cannot flow through the coronary arteries
The coronary arteries grow too narrow naturally with age

wrong_explanations.1: Heart muscle weakness (heart failure) is a consequence of CHD, not the cause — the cause is specifically fatty plaque build-up.
wrong_explanations.2: Blood viscosity can affect flow, but CHD is specifically about PLAQUE build-up in artery walls, not blood thickness.
wrong_explanations.3: Arteries don't naturally narrow with age — they narrow because of PLAQUES (fatty deposits) accumulating over time due to risk factors.

quiz.q: How does a stent treat coronary heart disease?
opts: It is inserted into the narrowed artery and expands to hold it open — restoring blood flow
It dissolves the fatty plaques blocking the artery
It reroutes blood around the blocked artery using a grafted vessel
It lowers blood cholesterol levels to prevent further plaque formation

wrong_explanations.1: Stents do not dissolve plaques — they physically hold the narrowed artery open.
wrong_explanations.2: Rerouting blood around a blockage using a grafted vessel = BYPASS SURGERY, not a stent.
wrong_explanations.3: Lowering cholesterol = STATINS (medication), not a stent.

quiz.q: Which of the following is a lifestyle risk factor for CHD that CAN be changed?
opts: Smoking cigarettes
Family history of heart disease (genetics)
Being male
Increasing age

wrong_explanations.1: Genetics is a risk factor — but you cannot change your DNA. It is a non-modifiable risk factor.
wrong_explanations.2: Sex (being male) slightly increases CHD risk, particularly at younger ages — but you cannot change this.
wrong_explanations.3: Age increases CHD risk, but everyone ages — you cannot avoid this risk factor.

foundation.common_mistake: A STENT holds a narrowed artery open — it does NOT bypass the blockage. A BYPASS SURGERY creates a new route around the blockage using a grafted blood vessel. Students often confuse these two treatments.

higher.higher_box: Statins lower LDL cholesterol — slowing fatty deposit formation in arteries. Stents keep coronary arteries open mechanically. Bypass surgery grafts a vessel (usually from the leg) around the blockage. Heart transplants replace the whole heart — require immunosuppression. Artificial hearts provide temporary support. Faulty valves can be replaced with biological or mechanical alternatives. Each treatment has risks (surgery complications, rejection, infection) and benefits to evaluate.

organisation/health-disease.summary: Define health and disease, distinguish communicable from non-communicable diseases, and explain risk factors.

quiz.q: What is the difference between a communicable and a non-communicable disease?
opts: Communicable diseases are caused by pathogens and can spread between organisms. Non-communicable diseases cannot spread.
Communicable diseases are always fatal. Non-communicable diseases are always mild.
Communicable diseases are caused by lifestyle. Non-communicable diseases are caused by pathogens.
They are the same thing — both terms describe infectious disease.

wrong_explanations.1: Neither type is always fatal or always mild — severity varies enormously in both categories.
wrong_explanations.2: This is exactly the wrong way round. Communicable = pathogens. Non-communicable = lifestyle, genetics, environment.
wrong_explanations.3: They are not the same — the key difference is whether the disease can be spread from person to person.

quiz.q: A study finds that people who eat more red meat have higher rates of bowel cancer. What does this show?
opts: A correlation between red meat consumption and bowel cancer — further research is needed to establish causation
Red meat DEFINITELY causes bowel cancer in everyone who eats it
Bowel cancer causes people to eat more red meat
This is meaningless data — correlations in diet studies are never reliable

wrong_explanations.1: Correlation does not automatically mean causation — the relationship could be due to other factors (e.g. diet quality overall, exercise levels).
wrong_explanations.2: This would be reverse causation — the data shows a link, but the disease developing before changing diet doesn't fit the timeline.
wrong_explanations.3: Epidemiological (population-based) data is valuable scientific evidence — correlation studies are an important first step in identifying risk factors.

quiz.q: Which of the following is a LIFESTYLE risk factor for non-communicable disease?
opts: Smoking cigarettes
Being 70 years old
Having a family history of heart disease
Living near a motorway (air pollution)

wrong_explanations.1: Age is a NON-LIFESTYLE (non-modifiable) risk factor — everyone ages and it cannot be changed.
wrong_explanations.2: Family history = GENETIC risk factor — also non-modifiable. You cannot choose your genes.
wrong_explanations.3: Living near a motorway = ENVIRONMENTAL risk factor — though this can sometimes be changed (by moving), it is generally classified as environmental rather than lifestyle.

foundation.common_mistake: A risk factor INCREASES the probability of getting a disease — it does NOT mean you WILL get it. A non-smoker can develop lung cancer; a heavy smoker might not. Risk factors change the statistical likelihood, not the certainty. Also — correlation does NOT always mean causation, though for many lifestyle risk factors, both have been established.

quiz.q: What is the difference between a communicable and a non-communicable disease?
opts: Communicable diseases are caused by pathogens and can spread between organisms. Non-communicable diseases cannot spread.
Communicable diseases are always fatal. Non-communicable diseases are always mild.
Communicable diseases are caused by lifestyle. Non-communicable diseases are caused by pathogens.
They are the same thing — both terms describe infectious disease.

wrong_explanations.1: Neither type is always fatal or always mild — severity varies enormously in both categories.
wrong_explanations.2: This is exactly the wrong way round. Communicable = pathogens. Non-communicable = lifestyle, genetics, environment.
wrong_explanations.3: They are not the same — the key difference is whether the disease can be spread from person to person.

higher.higher_box: Students should be able to evaluate the evidence that links lifestyle factors (smoking, obesity, alcohol, diet) to non-communicable disease. Correlation does not prove causation — consider confounding variables. Some risk factors are non-modifiable (genetics, age) while others are modifiable (lifestyle). Statistical analysis of large population data provides the strongest evidence.

organisation/cancer.summary: Explain how cancer develops, the difference between benign and malignant tumours, and how cancer is treated.

quiz.q: What is the key difference between a benign and a malignant tumour?
opts: Benign tumours stay in one place. Malignant tumours spread to other parts of the body (metastasis).
Benign tumours grow faster than malignant tumours.
Malignant tumours are caused by lifestyle; benign tumours are genetic.
Benign tumours require chemotherapy; malignant tumours only need surgery.

wrong_explanations.1: Malignant tumours typically grow FASTER and spread — benign tumours tend to grow more slowly and stay localised.
wrong_explanations.2: Both types can have genetic or lifestyle causes — the distinction is about whether the tumour SPREADS, not its cause.
wrong_explanations.3: This is the wrong way round — surgery is often used for localised (benign or early malignant) tumours. Chemotherapy is used for cancer that has spread.

quiz.q: How does UV radiation cause skin cancer?
opts: UV radiation damages DNA in skin cells — causing mutations in genes that control cell division
UV radiation directly kills healthy skin cells, forcing the body to replace them rapidly
UV radiation heats skin cells above their optimum temperature, causing them to mutate
UV radiation reduces the immune system's ability to detect and destroy abnormal cells

wrong_explanations.1: UV radiation doesn't kill healthy cells en masse — it damages DNA specifically. Rapid replacement due to damage is related to wound healing, not cancer.
wrong_explanations.2: UV radiation doesn't work by heat — it carries photons that directly interact with and damage DNA molecules.
wrong_explanations.3: UV radiation can slightly suppress immunity, but the primary cancer-causing mechanism is direct DNA mutation in skin cells.

quiz.q: Why does chemotherapy cause hair loss as a side effect?
opts: Chemotherapy targets all rapidly dividing cells — hair follicle cells divide rapidly, so they are also damaged
Chemotherapy drugs are toxic to the scalp skin only
The immune system attacks hair follicles as a reaction to chemotherapy drugs
Chemotherapy only causes hair loss when combined with radiotherapy

wrong_explanations.1: Chemotherapy drugs are not scalp-specific — they circulate in the blood and affect rapidly dividing cells throughout the whole body.
wrong_explanations.2: The immune reaction to chemotherapy doesn't specifically target hair follicles — the damage is from the drugs directly targeting dividing cells.
wrong_explanations.3: Hair loss can occur from chemotherapy alone — it is caused by the drug mechanism, not only in combination with radiotherapy.

foundation.common_mistake: BENIGN tumours stay in one place and are not cancer. MALIGNANT tumours spread (metastasis) and are cancer. Students often use 'tumour' and 'cancer' interchangeably — a benign tumour is NOT cancer. Also: chemotherapy targets ALL rapidly dividing cells, not just cancer cells — that's why it causes hair loss and immune suppression as side effects.

organisation/plant-tissues.summary: Describe the tissues found in plant organs and how they are adapted to their functions.

quiz.q: What is transported in xylem?
opts: Water and dissolved mineral ions — upwards from roots to leaves
Dissolved sugars from leaves to other parts of the plant
Oxygen produced by photosynthesis
Carbon dioxide for use in photosynthesis

wrong_explanations.1: Dissolved sugars = PHLOEM (translocation). Xylem carries water and minerals only.
wrong_explanations.2: Oxygen diffuses through air spaces in the spongy mesophyll and out through stomata — it doesn't travel in xylem.
wrong_explanations.3: CO₂ diffuses through stomata into air spaces — it doesn't travel in xylem.

quiz.q: Why are xylem cells dead?
opts: Dead cells have no contents — forming a completely hollow tube for water to flow through without obstruction
Xylem cells die because the lignin in their walls is toxic
Dead cells are lighter, helping water move upwards against gravity
Xylem cells die when the plant is no longer growing

wrong_explanations.1: Lignin is not toxic — it strengthens the walls. The cells die as part of their normal development, leaving behind a hollow lignified tube.
wrong_explanations.2: Dead cell content doesn't significantly change the weight of xylem — water moves upward due to the transpiration pull, not because of weight.
wrong_explanations.3: Xylem cells die as they mature during normal development, regardless of whether the plant is actively growing — this is a programmed developmental process.

quiz.q: What is the role of stomata in leaves?
opts: Allow CO₂ in for photosynthesis and O₂ out — also allow water vapour to escape during transpiration
Absorb water directly from the air into the leaf
Produce the waxy cuticle to reduce water loss
Store glucose produced by photosynthesis

wrong_explanations.1: Stomata can absorb some water vapour in humid conditions, but their primary function is gas EXCHANGE, not water absorption.
wrong_explanations.2: The waxy cuticle is produced by the EPIDERMIS cells — it is a secreted layer, not produced by stomata.
wrong_explanations.3: Glucose storage as starch happens in chloroplasts and other cells — stomata are pores for gas exchange.

foundation.common_mistake: Xylem transports WATER and MINERALS. Phloem transports SUGARS. Students regularly mix these up. A good memory trick: Xylem = water (X for H2O is a stretch, but think: Xylem goes up like water). Phloem = food (sugars). Also: xylem cells are DEAD; phloem cells are LIVING.

Students should be able to explain how the transpiration rate is measured using a potometer. Phloem translocation is driven by active loading of sucrose at source cells (using ATP from companion cells) creating a pressure gradient to sink cells.

organisation/transpiration.summary: Describe transpiration and the factors that affect its rate.

quiz.q: What is transpiration?
opts: The evaporation of water from the leaves of a plant through the stomata
The transport of dissolved sugars from leaves to the rest of the plant
The absorption of water by root hair cells from the soil
The process of photosynthesis producing water as a product

wrong_explanations.1: Transport of dissolved sugars = TRANSLOCATION (in phloem). Transpiration is specifically water evaporation from leaves.
wrong_explanations.2: Water absorption by roots = OSMOSIS through root hair cells. Transpiration is what happens at the LEAVES end.
wrong_explanations.3: Photosynthesis produces OXYGEN, not water — water is a REACTANT of photosynthesis (6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂).

quiz.q: A plant is moved from a humid greenhouse to a dry, windy environment. What happens to its transpiration rate?
opts: Transpiration increases significantly — lower humidity and wind both increase the rate
Transpiration decreases — the plant closes its stomata to conserve water
Transpiration stays the same — the two factors cancel each other out
Transpiration increases due to humidity but decreases due to wind — net effect is zero

wrong_explanations.1: The plant may eventually close stomata if it loses too much water — but the immediate effect of moving to dry, windy conditions is faster transpiration. Stomata closing is a response to stress, not an immediate automatic reaction.
wrong_explanations.2: The two factors don't cancel out — both low humidity AND wind INCREASE transpiration rate. They act in the same direction.
wrong_explanations.3: Both low humidity and wind INCREASE transpiration — humidity and wind both affect the concentration gradient for water vapour in the same way (they both maintain a steep gradient).

quiz.q: Why do plants transpire faster in brighter light?
opts: Brighter light causes stomata to open wider — allowing more water vapour to escape
Brighter light increases the temperature of the leaf, causing faster evaporation
Brighter light increases the rate of photosynthesis, which produces more water
Brighter light makes chlorophyll absorb water directly from the air

wrong_explanations.1: Brighter light does warm leaves slightly — but the PRIMARY mechanism is guard cells responding to light by opening stomata wider.
wrong_explanations.2: Photosynthesis uses water as a REACTANT — it consumes water, not produces it. Oxygen is the gas product.
wrong_explanations.3: Chlorophyll absorbs LIGHT energy — it does not absorb water from the air.

foundation.common_mistake: Transpiration is fastest in HOT, BRIGHT, DRY and WINDY conditions. Students often get humidity wrong — LOW humidity means DRY air which INCREASES transpiration rate (bigger concentration gradient for water vapour). HIGH humidity SLOWS transpiration. Think of it like this: dry air 'pulls' the water out of the leaf faster.

Vary one factor (temperature, humidity, light intensity, wind speed) while controlling others. Record distance air bubble moves per unit time. Calculate rate and plot a graph. Students should be able to design and evaluate such an investigation.

organisation/translocation.summary: Describe translocation and how it differs from transpiration.

quiz.q: What is the main substance transported in phloem?
opts: Sucrose (dissolved sugar) — from leaves to other parts of the plant
Water and dissolved mineral ions — from roots to leaves
Oxygen produced by photosynthesis
Carbon dioxide for use in photosynthesis

wrong_explanations.1: Water and minerals = XYLEM transport (transpiration stream). Phloem carries sugars.
wrong_explanations.2: Oxygen diffuses through air spaces and out through stomata — it is not transported in phloem.
wrong_explanations.3: CO₂ diffuses through stomata into the leaf — it is not transported in phloem.

quiz.q: In which direction does translocation move?
opts: Both upwards and downwards — from source (leaves) to any sink where sugar is needed
Upwards only — like the transpiration stream in xylem
Downwards only — gravity pulls the sugar solution down to the roots
Outwards only — from the centre of the stem to the leaf surfaces

wrong_explanations.1: Upwards only = TRANSPIRATION in XYLEM. Translocation in phloem moves in both directions depending on where the sink is.
wrong_explanations.2: If translocation were downwards only, growing shoot tips at the top of the plant could never receive sugars — but they clearly do.
wrong_explanations.3: Translocation moves along the length of the plant (up and down), not outwards from centre to leaf surface.

quiz.q: What is a 'sink' in the context of translocation?
opts: Any part of the plant where sugars are used or stored — e.g. roots, fruits, growing tips
The leaves — where sugars are produced by photosynthesis
The phloem vessels that transport sugars through the plant
The stomata — where water and gases are exchanged

wrong_explanations.1: The leaves are the SOURCE — where sugars are MADE. A sink is the DESTINATION where sugars are used or stored.
wrong_explanations.2: The phloem is the VESSEL — not the destination. Sinks are the organs that USE or STORE the sugars.
wrong_explanations.3: Stomata are gas exchange pores — they have no role in the source-sink relationship of translocation.

foundation.common_mistake: Translocation = SUGARS in PHLOEM. Transpiration = WATER in XYLEM. These are the two most commonly confused terms in plant biology. Translocation moves in BOTH directions — transpiration only goes UPWARDS. Phloem cells are LIVING — xylem cells are DEAD.

higher.higher_box: Active loading of sucrose into phloem sieve tubes at source cells (leaves) requires ATP from companion cells. This creates high osmotic pressure — water enters by osmosis from adjacent xylem, increasing hydrostatic pressure. The pressure gradient drives sucrose solution toward sink cells (roots, fruits, growing tips) where sucrose is actively unloaded — maintaining the gradient.

infection-response/communicable-diseases.summary: Describe what communicable diseases are, how pathogens spread and how the body defends itself.

quiz.q: What is the difference between a phagocyte and a lymphocyte?
opts: Phagocytes engulf pathogens non-specifically. Lymphocytes produce specific antibodies.
Phagocytes produce antibodies. Lymphocytes engulf pathogens.
Both engulf pathogens — they are the same type of white blood cell.
Phagocytes fight bacterial infections only. Lymphocytes fight viral infections only.

wrong_explanations.1: This is exactly the wrong way around. Phagocytes = engulf. Lymphocytes = antibodies.
wrong_explanations.2: Both are white blood cells but they have completely different functions — phagocytes engulf, lymphocytes make antibodies.
wrong_explanations.3: Both types of white blood cell work against a wide range of pathogens — they are not limited to one type of infection.

quiz.q: Why are memory cells important for immunity?
opts: They remain in the blood after infection and allow a rapid, large antibody response if the same pathogen is encountered again
They physically block pathogens from entering the bloodstream
They produce antibiotics to destroy bacterial infections
They replace damaged body cells after an infection has passed

wrong_explanations.1: Memory cells are immune cells — they don't form physical barriers. That role belongs to skin, mucus and cilia.
wrong_explanations.2: Antibiotics are medicines — they are not produced by any body cells. Memory cells produce antibodies, not antibiotics.
wrong_explanations.3: Cell replacement is done by stem cells and normal cell division — memory cells specifically remember and respond to specific pathogens.

quiz.q: How does the Anopheles mosquito spread malaria?
opts: It is a vector — it carries the Plasmodium protist and injects it into humans when it bites
The mosquito itself causes malaria — it is the pathogen
Mosquito bites break the skin, allowing malaria bacteria from the air to enter
The mosquito contaminates water sources with Plasmodium

wrong_explanations.1: The MOSQUITO is the vector — Plasmodium is the pathogen. The mosquito carries and transmits Plasmodium but does not cause the disease itself.
wrong_explanations.2: Malaria is caused by a PROTIST (Plasmodium) — not bacteria. And it is injected through the bite, not from the air.
wrong_explanations.3: Malaria is transmitted through bites — not through water. Cholera spreads through contaminated water.

quiz.q: Why does the body produce mucus in the airways?
opts: To trap inhaled pathogens and particles before they reach the lungs — cilia then sweep the mucus up to be swallowed
To provide a moist surface for gas exchange in the lungs
To produce antibodies that neutralise pathogens in the airways
To prevent the airways from drying out in cold weather

wrong_explanations.1: Gas exchange occurs in the ALVEOLI — the main function of mucus in the airways is trapping pathogens, not gas exchange.
wrong_explanations.2: Antibodies are produced by LYMPHOCYTES in the blood — not by mucus. Mucus is a physical/chemical barrier.
wrong_explanations.3: Moisture in the airways is a secondary benefit — the PRIMARY function of mucus is trapping pathogens and particles.

foundation.common_mistake: Antibiotics kill BACTERIA only — they have absolutely NO effect on viruses. Never say 'take antibiotics for a virus'. Flu is caused by a virus — antibiotics will not help. Also: phagocytes ENGULF pathogens. Lymphocytes PRODUCE ANTIBODIES. These are different white blood cells with different jobs.

The mosquito itself causes malaria — it is the pathogen
Mosquito bites break the skin, allowing malaria bacteria from the air to enter
The mosquito contaminates water sources with Plasmodium

wrong_explanations.1: The MOSQUITO is the vector — Plasmodium is the pathogen. The mosquito carries and transmits Plasmodium but does not cause the disease itself.
wrong_explanations.2: Malaria is caused by a PROTIST (Plasmodium) — not bacteria. And it is injected through the bite, not from the air.
wrong_explanations.3: Malaria is transmitted through bites — not through water. Cholera spreads through contaminated water.

quiz.q: Why does the body produce mucus in the airways?
opts: To trap inhaled pathogens and particles before they reach the lungs — cilia then sweep the mucus up to be swallowed
To provide a moist surface for gas exchange in the lungs
To produce antibodies that neutralise pathogens in the airways
To prevent the airways from drying out in cold weather

wrong_explanations.1: Gas exchange occurs in the ALVEOLI — the main function of mucus in the airways is trapping pathogens, not gas exchange.
wrong_explanations.2: Antibodies are produced by LYMPHOCYTES in the blood — not by mucus. Mucus is a physical/chemical barrier.
wrong_explanations.3: Moisture in the airways is a secondary benefit — the PRIMARY function of mucus is trapping pathogens and particles.

higher.common_mistake: Antibiotics kill BACTERIA only — they have absolutely NO effect on viruses. Never say 'take antibiotics for a virus'. Flu is caused by a virus — antibiotics will not help. Also: phagocytes ENGULF pathogens. Lymphocytes PRODUCE ANTIBODIES. These are different white blood cells with different jobs.
higher.higher_box: The secondary immune response is faster and larger than the primary — memory B-cells rapidly differentiate into plasma cells producing large quantities of specific antibodies. This is the basis of vaccination and acquired immunity. Students should be able to explain how memory cells allow rapid response on re-exposure to a specific pathogen.

infection-response/viral-diseases.summary: Describe the main viral diseases — measles, HIV/AIDS and tobacco mosaic virus.

quiz.q: Why does HIV lead to AIDS?
opts: HIV destroys T-lymphocytes — the immune system becomes too weak to fight off infections
HIV produces toxins that directly damage all organs simultaneously
HIV causes the body to overproduce white blood cells, overwhelming the system
HIV converts into the AIDS virus after several years in the body

wrong_explanations.1: HIV specifically targets immune cells — it doesn't produce toxins that damage other organs directly.
wrong_explanations.2: HIV destroys white blood cells (lymphocytes) — it does not cause overproduction.
wrong_explanations.3: HIV doesn't 'convert' into AIDS — AIDS is the CONDITION resulting from immune system collapse caused by HIV destroying lymphocytes.

quiz.q: How does tobacco mosaic virus affect plant growth?
opts: It disrupts chlorophyll production — leaves develop mosaic discolouration and photosynthesise less efficiently
It blocks the xylem, preventing water reaching the leaves
It causes root cells to die, so the plant cannot absorb minerals
It produces toxins that kill plant cells directly

wrong_explanations.1: TMV affects leaf cells and chlorophyll — not the xylem. The reduced photosynthesis from less chlorophyll is the key effect.
wrong_explanations.2: TMV infects leaf cells, not root cells. Mineral absorption is unaffected directly.
wrong_explanations.3: TMV disrupts chlorophyll production in leaf cells — it doesn't produce toxins that kill cells outright.

quiz.q: A measles patient is told to rest and take paracetamol. Why are they not given antibiotics?
opts: Measles is caused by a virus — antibiotics only kill bacteria and have no effect on viruses
Antibiotics would make the rash worse
The patient is too young for antibiotics
Paracetamol is stronger than antibiotics

wrong_explanations.1: Rash worsening from antibiotics is not a reason — the fundamental reason is that antibiotics target bacterial cell walls and structures that viruses simply don't have.
wrong_explanations.2: Age is not the deciding factor — the pathogen type is. Antibiotics don't work on viruses regardless of patient age.
wrong_explanations.3: Paracetamol relieves symptoms — it does not treat the infection. Antibiotics treat bacterial infections — but measles is viral.

foundation.common_mistake: HIV and AIDS are NOT the same thing. HIV is the VIRUS. AIDS is the CONDITION that develops when HIV has destroyed enough lymphocytes that the immune system collapses. You can have HIV for many years without having AIDS if treated with ARVs. Also: antibiotics DO NOT treat viral infections — not measles, not HIV, not flu.
