infection-response/bacterial-diseases.summary: Describe Salmonella food poisoning and Gonorrhoea as examples of bacterial diseases.

quiz.q: How is Salmonella food poisoning prevented?
opts: Thorough cooking to kill bacteria, good food hygiene, refrigeration and hand washing
Taking antibiotics before eating suspected food
Avoiding all animal products entirely
Vaccinating all humans against Salmonella

wrong_explanations.1: Taking antibiotics preventatively is dangerous — it promotes antibiotic resistance without benefit.
wrong_explanations.2: Thorough cooking makes animal products safe — you don't need to avoid them entirely.
wrong_explanations.3: Human Salmonella vaccines are not routinely used — poultry vaccination is used in the UK to reduce the source.

quiz.q: Why is gonorrhoea becoming harder to treat?
opts: Antibiotic-resistant strains have evolved — bacteria with resistance mutations survive antibiotic treatment and pass on resistance genes
Gonorrhoea has mutated into a virus, which antibiotics cannot target
New strains produce proteins that destroy antibiotic molecules before they can work
People have developed immunity to the antibiotics used to treat it

wrong_explanations.1: Gonorrhoea remains a bacterium — bacteria cannot evolve into viruses. The issue is antibiotic resistance in bacterial populations.
wrong_explanations.2: Some resistant bacteria do produce enzymes (like beta-lactamases) that break down antibiotics — this is one mechanism of resistance, but the question is about the broader process of natural selection producing resistant strains.
wrong_explanations.3: People develop immunity to pathogens, not to medicines. Antibiotic resistance is a property of the BACTERIA, not of human patients.

foundation.common_mistake: Gonorrhoea IS treatable with antibiotics — but antibiotic-resistant strains are emerging. Do not say 'gonorrhoea cannot be treated'. Say instead: 'it can be treated, but resistance is a growing problem'. Also: Salmonella causes disease primarily through TOXINS — not just by the bacteria damaging cells directly.

infection-response/fungal-protist-diseas.summary: Describe rose black spot (fungal) and malaria (protist) as examples of these disease types.

quiz.q: Why does rose black spot cause poor plant growth?
opts: Infected leaves develop spots and fall off — less leaf area means less photosynthesis and less glucose for growth
The fungus blocks the xylem, preventing water reaching the leaves
The fungus produces toxins that directly kill root cells
Spores block stomata, preventing CO₂ from entering the leaf

wrong_explanations.1: Rose black spot infects LEAF CELLS — it doesn't block xylem. The impact is through reduced photosynthetic capacity.
wrong_explanations.2: Rose black spot is a leaf disease — it doesn't specifically target roots.
wrong_explanations.3: Blocking stomata is not the primary mechanism — the main effect is leaf loss through defoliation reducing total photosynthesis.

quiz.q: What is the role of the Anopheles mosquito in malaria?
opts: It is the vector — it carries Plasmodium from one human to another when it bites
It is the pathogen — the mosquito itself causes the disease
It injects its own saliva which contains the fever-causing toxin
It is only involved in spreading malaria in tropical countries — not elsewhere

wrong_explanations.1: The PATHOGEN is Plasmodium (the protist). The mosquito is the VECTOR — it carries and transmits the pathogen without being the cause of disease itself.
wrong_explanations.2: Mosquito saliva can cause local irritation — but the disease malaria is caused specifically by Plasmodium being injected, not by the saliva itself.
wrong_explanations.3: Malaria is predominantly tropical because Anopheles mosquitoes thrive in warm, wet environments — but the mosquito's role as vector is the same wherever it lives.

foundation.common_mistake: The MOSQUITO is the VECTOR for malaria — not the pathogen. Plasmodium (the protist) is the pathogen that causes disease. The mosquito carries and transmits it. This distinction is a very common exam question. Also: rose black spot is caused by a FUNGUS — not bacteria, not a virus.

infection-response/vaccination.summary: Explain how vaccines work, why they protect populations and what herd immunity means.

quiz.q: How does a vaccine protect against future infection?
opts: It stimulates an immune response and produces memory cells — so the body responds rapidly if the real pathogen invades
It kills any pathogen that enters the body for the rest of your life
It strengthens all aspects of the immune system permanently
It prevents the pathogen from ever entering the body

wrong_explanations.1: Vaccines don't provide a permanent killing effect — they prepare MEMORY CELLS that respond quickly if the pathogen arrives.
wrong_explanations.2: Vaccines provide SPECIFIC immunity to one pathogen — they don't generally boost the whole immune system.
wrong_explanations.3: Pathogens can still enter the body after vaccination — the vaccine means the immune system destroys them so quickly they don't cause disease.

quiz.q: What is herd immunity?
opts: When enough of a population is immune that the pathogen cannot spread effectively — protecting even unvaccinated individuals
When every single person in a population has been vaccinated
When an entire herd of animals is vaccinated against a disease
When a person becomes naturally immune after recovering from a disease

wrong_explanations.1: Herd immunity doesn't require 100% vaccination — it just needs enough people to be immune to break the chain of transmission.
wrong_explanations.2: 'Herd' refers to any population — not just animal herds. Herd immunity applies to human populations too.
wrong_explanations.3: Natural immunity after infection DOES contribute to herd immunity — but vaccination is the safer way to achieve it without suffering the disease.

quiz.q: Why is the flu vaccine updated every year?
opts: The influenza virus mutates rapidly — its surface antigens change, so last year's vaccine may no longer match this year's strains
Memory cells from flu vaccines only last one year before disappearing
Flu vaccines contain live viruses that must be freshly prepared each season
The government changes the vaccine composition each year as a precaution

wrong_explanations.1: Memory cells from vaccines can last many years or a lifetime — the issue is not memory cell lifespan but viral mutation.
wrong_explanations.2: Many flu vaccines use inactivated (killed) viruses — not live ones. The reason for annual updates is viral mutation.
wrong_explanations.3: The composition is updated based on scientific surveillance of which influenza strains are circulating — it's not an arbitrary precaution.

foundation.common_mistake: Vaccines do NOT give you the disease. They contain harmless forms of the pathogen (dead, weakened or just antigens) — they cannot cause the actual disease. The immune response to the vaccine (mild fever, soreness) is your body's NORMAL reaction to stimulation — not the disease itself.

The immune response to the vaccine (mild fever, soreness) is your body's NORMAL reaction to stimulation — not the disease itself.

Students should be able to evaluate data on vaccination programmes and the incidence of disease, including interpreting graphs of disease cases before and after vaccination introduction. mRNA vaccines (e.g. COVID-19) instruct cells to produce a viral antigen, triggering an immune response without using any viral material.

infection-response/antibiotics-painkille.summary: Explain how antibiotics work, why antibiotic resistance is a threat, and the role of painkillers.

quiz.q: Why can't antibiotics treat viral infections?
opts: Viruses don't have cell walls or bacterial ribosomes — the structures antibiotics target don't exist in viruses
Viruses are too small for antibiotics to reach
Antibiotics are absorbed too slowly to reach viruses in the bloodstream
Viruses produce enzymes that destroy antibiotics

wrong_explanations.1: Viruses are smaller than bacteria — but size is not why antibiotics don't work. Antibiotics target specific bacterial structures that viruses simply don't have.
wrong_explanations.2: Antibiotics are absorbed into the bloodstream — but they have no biological target in virus particles, so reaching them makes no difference.
wrong_explanations.3: Some resistant bacteria do produce enzymes that destroy antibiotics — but this is a bacterial resistance mechanism, not a general viral property.

quiz.q: Why is it important to always complete a full course of antibiotics?
opts: Stopping early leaves the most resistant bacteria alive — they survive and reproduce, increasing resistance
Stopping early means the antibiotics already taken are wasted and have no effect
Partial courses make the bacteria grow faster as a response
It doesn't matter — stopping early is fine if symptoms improve

wrong_explanations.1: The antibiotics already taken do have effect — they kill susceptible bacteria. The problem is that the resistant survivors are left to reproduce.
wrong_explanations.2: Bacteria don't 'grow faster' in response to antibiotics stopping — the issue is selective survival of resistant variants.
wrong_explanations.3: This is a dangerous misconception — symptoms improving means the immune system and antibiotics are working, but bacteria may still be present. Stopping early increases resistance risk.

foundation.common_mistake: Painkillers do NOT treat infections — they only relieve symptoms. A patient taking only paracetamol for a bacterial infection is NOT treating the infection. Also: always complete antibiotic courses — stopping early is a major driver of resistance because the bacteria that survive are likely to be the more resistant ones.

MRSA is a clinically important example. Students should be able to explain why reducing unnecessary antibiotic use and completing courses slows resistance development.

infection-response/drug-discovery-develo.summary: Describe how new drugs are discovered, tested and approved before being used on patients.

quiz.q: What is a double-blind clinical trial?
opts: Neither the patients nor the doctors know who is receiving the real drug or the placebo — eliminates bias from both sides
Only the patients don't know which treatment they are receiving
The trial uses two different drugs tested simultaneously against each other
The trial is conducted in the dark to prevent visual identification of the drug

wrong_explanations.1: If only the PATIENT doesn't know = single-blind. Double-blind means BOTH patient AND doctor are unaware — this prevents the doctor's expectations influencing how they assess patient progress.
wrong_explanations.2: A trial testing two real drugs against each other is a comparative trial — double-blind refers to who knows about treatment allocation, not how many drugs are tested.
wrong_explanations.3: 'Blind' in a clinical context means 'unaware of treatment allocation' — it has nothing to do with actual darkness.

quiz.q: Why is a placebo used in clinical trials?
opts: To control for the placebo effect — some patients improve just because they believe they are being treated
To give patients a low starting dose before they receive the real drug
To ensure every patient receives some form of medical benefit during the trial
To test the side effects of inactive substances

wrong_explanations.1: A placebo is not a low dose — it is INACTIVE and contains no active ingredient.
wrong_explanations.2: Patients in the placebo group receive no active treatment — this is ethically managed carefully and only used when ethically appropriate.
wrong_explanations.3: Placebos by definition have no pharmacological activity — any side effects are psychological or coincidental.

quiz.q: What was the key lesson from the thalidomide tragedy?
opts: Drugs must be rigorously tested for all potential effects including effects on embryos — testing requirements were significantly strengthened
Natural drugs are always safer than synthetic ones
Sedatives should never be prescribed during pregnancy
Clinical trials are unnecessary if a drug has been shown to be safe in adults

wrong_explanations.1: Thalidomide was a synthetic drug — but many synthetic drugs are completely safe. The lesson was specifically about testing for effects on ALL groups, especially embryos.
wrong_explanations.2: The lesson was specifically about testing for ALL effects in ALL populations — not a blanket ban on sedatives in pregnancy.
wrong_explanations.3: The thalidomide case showed exactly WHY clinical trials must be thorough — an adult-safe drug was devastating to embryos.

Clinical trial phases: Phase 1 (safety, healthy volunteers), Phase 2 (efficacy, patients), Phase 3 (large-scale, double-blind, placebo-controlled). Double-blind eliminates bias from both patients AND doctors. Peer review validates findings before publication. Thalidomide's withdrawal led to much stricter testing requirements, particularly for testing effects on embryo development.

bioenergetics/photosynthesis.summary: Describe photosynthesis — what it is, where it happens and what the equation means.

quiz.q: What is the word equation for photosynthesis?
opts: Carbon dioxide + water → glucose + oxygen
Glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water
Carbon dioxide + glucose → water + oxygen
Water + oxygen → carbon dioxide + glucose

wrong_explanations.1: That is the equation for AEROBIC RESPIRATION — the exact reverse of photosynthesis.
wrong_explanations.2: CO₂ and glucose are never on the same side. CO₂ is a reactant; glucose is a product of photosynthesis.
wrong_explanations.3: Oxygen is a PRODUCT — it is produced by photosynthesis and released. It doesn't go in.

quiz.q: Why do plants appear green?
opts: Chlorophyll reflects green light — it absorbs red and blue wavelengths instead
Plants produce green light as a by-product of photosynthesis
Green light is needed most for photosynthesis so it is absorbed
All plant cells are filled with green-coloured water

wrong_explanations.1: Plants don't produce or emit light — they only reflect it. Green is the colour reflected, not produced.
wrong_explanations.2: Green is actually the LEAST useful wavelength for photosynthesis — red and blue are absorbed most efficiently.
wrong_explanations.3: Cell sap in vacuoles is usually colourless — the green colour comes from chlorophyll in chloroplasts.

quiz.q: In a dark room, does a plant carry out photosynthesis?
opts: No — photosynthesis requires light energy and cannot occur in darkness
Yes — plants always photosynthesise, day and night
Yes — plants store light energy during the day and use it at night
Only partially — stomata close so photosynthesis slows but doesn't stop

wrong_explanations.1: Respiration occurs day and night — but PHOTOSYNTHESIS specifically requires light energy. Without light, it stops completely.
wrong_explanations.2: Plants do store glucose — but photosynthesis itself cannot run without light. The stored glucose is used in respiration at night.
wrong_explanations.3: Stomata closing does reduce gas exchange in the dark, but photosynthesis stops specifically because there is no light energy, not because stomata are closed.

quiz.q: Where exactly in a plant cell does photosynthesis take place?
opts: Chloroplasts — which contain the chlorophyll pigment that absorbs light
Mitochondria — which produce the energy needed for photosynthesis
The nucleus — which contains the genes controlling photosynthesis
The vacuole — which stores the raw materials for photosynthesis

wrong_explanations.1: Mitochondria are the site of RESPIRATION — they produce ATP energy but do not carry out photosynthesis.
wrong_explanations.2: The nucleus contains the DNA instructions but is not the location where photosynthesis reactions actually occur.
wrong_explanations.3: The vacuole stores cell sap (sugars and salts) — it is not involved in carrying out photosynthesis.

foundation.common_mistake: Photosynthesis only happens in LIGHT and only in cells with CHLOROPLASTS. Respiration happens ALL THE TIME in ALL living cells. Never say 'plants don't respire' — they do, constantly. In bright light they photosynthesise faster than they respire, which is why we see net O₂ production.

quiz.q: What is the word equation for photosynthesis?
opts: Carbon dioxide + water → glucose + oxygen
Glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water
Carbon dioxide + glucose → water + oxygen
Water + oxygen → carbon dioxide + glucose

wrong_explanations.1: That is the equation for AEROBIC RESPIRATION — the exact reverse of photosynthesis.
wrong_explanations.2: CO₂ and glucose are never on the same side. CO₂ is a reactant; glucose is a product of photosynthesis.
wrong_explanations.3: Oxygen is a PRODUCT — it is produced by photosynthesis and released. It doesn't go in.

quiz.q: Why do plants appear green?
opts: Chlorophyll reflects green light — it absorbs red and blue wavelengths instead
Plants produce green light as a by-product of photosynthesis
Green light is needed most for photosynthesis so it is absorbed
All plant cells are filled with green-coloured water

wrong_explanations.1: Plants don't produce or emit light — they only reflect it. Green is the colour reflected, not produced.
wrong_explanations.2: Green is actually the LEAST useful wavelength for photosynthesis — red and blue are absorbed most efficiently.
wrong_explanations.3: Cell sap in vacuoles is usually colourless — the green colour comes from chlorophyll in chloroplasts.

quiz.q: In a dark room, does a plant carry out photosynthesis?
opts: No — photosynthesis requires light energy and cannot occur in darkness
Yes — plants always photosynthesise, day and night
Yes — plants store light energy during the day and use it at night
Only partially — stomata close so photosynthesis slows but doesn't stop

wrong_explanations.1: Respiration occurs day and night — but PHOTOSYNTHESIS specifically requires light energy. Without light, it stops completely.
wrong_explanations.2: Plants do store glucose — but photosynthesis itself cannot run without light. The stored glucose is used in respiration at night.
wrong_explanations.3: Stomata closing does reduce gas exchange in the dark, but photosynthesis stops specifically because there is no light energy, not because stomata are closed.

quiz.q: Where exactly in a plant cell does photosynthesis take place?
opts: Chloroplasts — which contain the chlorophyll pigment that absorbs light
Mitochondria — which produce the energy needed for photosynthesis
The nucleus — which contains the genes controlling photosynthesis
The vacuole — which stores the raw materials for photosynthesis

wrong_explanations.1: Mitochondria are the site of RESPIRATION — they produce ATP energy but do not carry out photosynthesis.
wrong_explanations.2: The nucleus contains the DNA instructions but is not the location where photosynthesis reactions actually occur.
wrong_explanations.3: The vacuole stores cell sap (sugars and salts) — it is not involved in carrying out photosynthesis.

higher.common_mistake: Photosynthesis only happens in LIGHT and only in cells with CHLOROPLASTS. Respiration happens ALL THE TIME in ALL living cells. Never say 'plants don't respire' — they do, constantly. In bright light they photosynthesise faster than they respire, which is why we see net O₂ production.

bioenergetics/rate-of-photosynthesis.summary: Explain what limits the rate of photosynthesis and how to investigate it.

quiz.q: A plant is given very bright light and high CO₂. The rate of photosynthesis stops increasing. What is now the most likely limiting factor?
opts: Temperature — enzyme activity is now the bottleneck
Light intensity — the plant needs even more light
Carbon dioxide — the plant needs more CO₂
The plant has used up all its chlorophyll

wrong_explanations.1: Light is already very bright — it is NOT the limiting factor.
wrong_explanations.2: CO₂ is already high — it is NOT the limiting factor.
wrong_explanations.3: Chlorophyll is not used up — it is a pigment that absorbs light and is not consumed. Another factor is now limiting the rate.

quiz.q: In an experiment, moving a lamp further from an aquatic plant reduces the bubble count. What does this show?
opts: Light intensity is the limiting factor at that distance — reducing it reduces the rate of photosynthesis
The plant needs to be closer to warm water
CO₂ levels decrease as the lamp moves further away
The plant becomes stressed when the lamp moves

wrong_explanations.1: The lamp produces light, not heat — the water bath controls temperature.
wrong_explanations.2: Moving a lamp doesn't change CO₂ levels — CO₂ comes from the water/air around the plant.
wrong_explanations.3: Plants don't have a stress response in the same way animals do — the reduced bubble rate is a direct physical consequence of reduced light energy.

quiz.q: Why does photosynthesis rate decrease at temperatures above the optimum?
opts: Enzymes in the chloroplast denature — their active sites change shape permanently and can no longer function
The plant closes its stomata to prevent water loss in the heat
High temperatures cause chlorophyll to break down permanently
More CO₂ evaporates from the leaf at higher temperatures

wrong_explanations.1: Stomata closing reduces CO₂ entry — but the primary reason for rate decrease above optimum temperature is ENZYME DENATURATION.
wrong_explanations.2: Chlorophyll can be affected by extreme heat, but at typical above-optimum temperatures (e.g. 45–50°C) the main limiting effect is enzyme denaturation.
wrong_explanations.3: CO₂ is a gas that dissolves in water — it doesn't 'evaporate from the leaf'. The CO₂ supply issue is about stomata and diffusion rates.

foundation.common_mistake: When a graph of photosynthesis rate levels off (plateaus), students often say 'the plant has run out of chlorophyll' or 'there is no more light'. The correct answer is that ANOTHER FACTOR has become limiting — usually CO₂ or temperature. The plant hasn't run out of anything — a different factor is now the bottleneck.

If a plant is 10 cm from the lamp and moved to 20 cm, light intensity drops to 1/4. Students should be able to apply this to RP5 data and interpret rate vs light intensity graphs — explaining plateau regions as evidence of another limiting factor.

bioenergetics/uses-of-glucose.summary: Describe how plants use the glucose produced by photosynthesis.

quiz.q: Why do plants store glucose as starch rather than keeping it as glucose?
opts: Starch is insoluble — it doesn't affect osmosis or water potential inside cells
Starch contains more energy per molecule than glucose
Starch is easier to transport around the plant than glucose
Glucose would be eaten by bacteria if stored directly

wrong_explanations.1: Glucose and starch (a polymer of glucose) contain the same energy per gram — starch isn't more energy-dense.
wrong_explanations.2: It's actually GLUCOSE that is transported in the phloem (as sucrose) — not starch. Starch is specifically an insoluble store.
wrong_explanations.3: Bacteria can break down starch too — the reason is osmosis, not bacterial protection.

quiz.q: A plant is grown in soil with no nitrate ions. Which molecule will it struggle to make?
opts: Proteins — amino acids need nitrogen (from nitrates) to form the amino group
Glucose — nitrates are needed for photosynthesis
Starch — nitrate ions are needed to convert glucose to starch
Cellulose — cell walls require nitrogen to polymerise glucose

wrong_explanations.1: Glucose is produced by photosynthesis using CO₂ and water — nitrates are not needed.
wrong_explanations.2: Starch is made from glucose polymerisation — no nitrogen is needed for this process.
wrong_explanations.3: Cellulose is made from glucose joined by glycosidic bonds — no nitrogen involved.

quiz.q: What is the main use of glucose in plants?
opts: Aerobic respiration — releasing energy (ATP) to power all cell processes
Making starch — all glucose is stored for later use
Building cell walls from cellulose — the plant's primary structural need
Making proteins — converting glucose to amino acids

wrong_explanations.1: Not all glucose is stored — plants use glucose continuously for growth, which means some becomes cellulose, but energy supply via respiration is the primary ongoing use.
wrong_explanations.2: Cellulose is important but only for cell walls — energy supply through respiration is the primary ongoing requirement.
wrong_explanations.3: Protein synthesis is important, but requires nitrogen — not all glucose goes to proteins.

foundation.common_mistake: Starch is stored instead of glucose because starch is INSOLUBLE — it doesn't affect osmosis. Glucose is soluble and would draw water into cells by osmosis if it accumulated. Students often say 'starch is easier to digest' — that's not the reason plants store starch instead of glucose.

bioenergetics/aerobic-respiration.summary: Describe aerobic respiration — what it is, where it happens and what it produces.

quiz.q: Where in the cell does aerobic respiration take place?
opts: Mitochondria
Chloroplasts
Nucleus
Ribosomes

wrong_explanations.1: Chloroplasts carry out PHOTOSYNTHESIS — they capture energy from light. Mitochondria RELEASE energy from glucose.
wrong_explanations.2: The nucleus contains DNA and controls the cell — it is not the site of aerobic respiration.
wrong_explanations.3: Ribosomes build proteins — they are not involved in energy release from glucose.

quiz.q: What is the difference between respiration and breathing?
opts: Breathing moves air in and out of the lungs. Respiration is the chemical process in cells that releases energy from glucose.
They are the same process — both refer to taking in oxygen and releasing carbon dioxide.
Breathing produces ATP. Respiration produces oxygen.
Respiration only occurs in the lungs. Breathing occurs throughout the body.

wrong_explanations.1: Breathing and respiration are NOT the same — confusing them is one of the most common biology mistakes. Breathing is a mechanical process; respiration is a chemical one.
wrong_explanations.2: Breathing takes in air (including O₂) — but ATP is produced by RESPIRATION in cells. Oxygen is produced by photosynthesis, not breathing.
wrong_explanations.3: Respiration occurs in ALL living cells throughout the body — not just the lungs.

quiz.q: Why do muscle cells contain more mitochondria than skin cells?
opts: Muscle cells need much more ATP energy for contraction — more mitochondria produce more ATP
Mitochondria are heavier than other organelles and sink to the bottom of active cells
Skin cells are too small to contain many mitochondria
Muscle cells use more oxygen, so they need more mitochondria to absorb it

wrong_explanations.1: Organelles don't 'sink' — they are distributed throughout the cytoplasm based on cellular need.
wrong_explanations.2: Skin cells are actually similar in size to muscle cells — it's energy demand, not size, that determines mitochondria number.
wrong_explanations.3: Mitochondria do use oxygen in respiration — but they use it to produce ATP. The driving factor for more mitochondria is the ENERGY DEMAND, not simply oxygen availability.

foundation.common_mistake: RESPIRATION is not the same as BREATHING. Breathing is ventilation — moving air in and out of lungs. Respiration is the CHEMICAL PROCESS in cells that releases energy from glucose. Also: aerobic respiration happens in MITOCHONDRIA — not in the nucleus, not in chloroplasts.

Nucleus
Ribosomes

higher.higher_box: ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is the immediate energy currency of cells — it directly powers muscle contraction, active transport, protein synthesis and cell division. Aerobic respiration produces ~36–38 ATP per glucose molecule. This compares to only ~2 ATP from anaerobic respiration — which is why aerobic respiration is much more efficient and is used for sustained activity.

bioenergetics/anaerobic-respiration.summary: Describe anaerobic respiration in animals and yeast and explain its uses.

quiz.q: What is produced by anaerobic respiration in yeast?
opts: Ethanol and carbon dioxide
Lactic acid
Glucose and oxygen
Carbon dioxide and water

wrong_explanations.1: Lactic acid is produced by ANIMAL muscles during anaerobic respiration — NOT by yeast. Yeast produces ethanol + CO₂.
wrong_explanations.2: Glucose and oxygen are the REACTANTS in aerobic respiration — they go in, not come out of anaerobic respiration.
wrong_explanations.3: CO₂ and water = products of AEROBIC respiration. Anaerobic respiration in yeast produces ethanol + CO₂ (not water).

quiz.q: Why does bread dough rise when yeast is added?
opts: Yeast ferments sugars in the dough — the CO₂ produced forms bubbles that make the dough expand
Yeast produces oxygen which inflates the dough
Yeast absorbs water from the dough, making it lighter
Yeast produces lactic acid which reacts with flour to create gas

wrong_explanations.1: Yeast produces CO₂ and ethanol during fermentation — not oxygen. The ethanol evaporates when the bread is baked.
wrong_explanations.2: Yeast absorbs sugars for fermentation — it doesn't absorb significant water from the dough.
wrong_explanations.3: Yeast (not animals) in bread produces ethanol and CO₂ — not lactic acid.

quiz.q: During a sprint race, an athlete's muscles switch to anaerobic respiration. Why?
opts: The muscles are working so hard that oxygen cannot be delivered fast enough — anaerobic respiration continues without it
Anaerobic respiration produces more ATP, so athletes switch to it for maximum performance
The lungs stop absorbing oxygen during intense exercise
Glucose runs out during sprinting so the muscles use a different fuel

wrong_explanations.1: Anaerobic respiration produces FAR LESS ATP (~2 vs ~36) — it is not chosen for maximum performance. It is an emergency fallback when oxygen supply is insufficient.
wrong_explanations.2: The lungs work harder during exercise, not less — breathing rate and depth increase. But even this can't supply enough oxygen for maximum muscle output.
wrong_explanations.3: Muscles use glucose during sprinting — glycogen stores are broken down to supply glucose. Glucose shortage ('hitting the wall') is different from the short-term switch to anaerobic respiration.

quiz.q: Which process produces more ATP per glucose molecule?
opts: Aerobic respiration — approximately 36–38 ATP compared to 2 ATP from anaerobic
Anaerobic respiration — it happens faster so produces more total ATP
They produce the same amount — both start with glucose
Anaerobic respiration — the lactic acid contains extra energy

wrong_explanations.1: Anaerobic can produce ATP FASTER per unit time in emergencies — but produces FAR LESS per glucose molecule.
wrong_explanations.2: They both start with glucose but have completely different efficiencies. Aerobic: ~36–38 ATP. Anaerobic: ~2 ATP.
wrong_explanations.3: Lactic acid does retain some chemical energy (glucose isn't fully broken down) — but from the cell's perspective, anaerobic yields only ~2 ATP of USABLE energy.

foundation.common_mistake: Anaerobic respiration in ANIMALS produces LACTIC ACID. Anaerobic respiration in YEAST produces ETHANOL + CO₂. These are different products — this distinction comes up repeatedly in exams. Do NOT say 'lactic acid' when asked about yeast fermentation.

bioenergetics/response-to-exercise.summary: Describe how the body responds during and after exercise, including oxygen debt.

quiz.q: Why does heart rate increase during exercise?
opts: To deliver more oxygen and glucose to muscles and remove CO₂ and lactic acid faster
To raise body temperature to improve enzyme efficiency
To produce more red blood cells for the increased demand
To increase blood pressure so more oxygen enters through the skin

wrong_explanations.1: Exercise does raise body temperature slightly — but this is a consequence, not the purpose of increased heart rate.
wrong_explanations.2: New red blood cells take days to produce — the immediate response is to pump existing blood faster.
wrong_explanations.3: Oxygen doesn't enter through the skin in humans — it enters through the lungs. The skin's increased blood flow is for COOLING, not oxygen absorption.

quiz.q: Why do you continue to breathe heavily for several minutes after stopping intense exercise?
opts: Repaying the oxygen debt — extra O₂ is needed to convert accumulated lactic acid back to glucose in the liver
The lungs take time to slow down after being stimulated
To replace all the oxygen used during exercise
Lactic acid in the blood stimulates the lungs to keep working hard

wrong_explanations.1: The lungs respond to chemical signals from the blood — they slow down when CO₂ and lactic acid levels normalise.
wrong_explanations.2: All the oxygen used during exercise was already replaced during exercise — the post-exercise breathing is specifically about the lactic acid that accumulated.
wrong_explanations.3: Lactic acid in the blood does stimulate breathing — but the reason breathing stays elevated is specifically to process that lactic acid in the liver.

quiz.q: What is oxygen debt?
opts: The extra oxygen needed after exercise to convert accumulated lactic acid back to glucose in the liver
The oxygen missing from muscle cells during intense exercise
The difference between oxygen inhaled and oxygen used during exercise
The oxygen stored in myoglobin in muscles before exercise

wrong_explanations.1: The lack of oxygen in muscles during exercise is what CAUSES anaerobic respiration — oxygen debt specifically refers to the post-exercise oxygen requirement to process the resulting lactic acid.
wrong_explanations.2: Difference between inhaled and used oxygen relates to metabolic efficiency — oxygen debt is specifically post-exercise processing of lactic acid.
wrong_explanations.3: Myoglobin stores oxygen in muscles — this is used during exercise, but 'oxygen debt' refers to the post-exercise recovery process.

foundation.common_mistake: Oxygen debt is NOT the lack of oxygen in the blood during exercise. It is the EXTRA oxygen needed AFTER exercise to convert the lactic acid that accumulated. Students often say 'you breathe harder during exercise to repay oxygen debt' — you breathe harder DURING exercise to supply O₂, and continue breathing hard AFTER to repay the debt.

bioenergetics/metabolism.summary: Define metabolism and describe the key metabolic reactions in living organisms.

quiz.q: What is metabolism?
opts: The sum of all chemical reactions taking place in a cell or organism
Only the reactions that release energy — such as respiration
The process of digesting food in the gut
How quickly a person breathes and moves

wrong_explanations.1: Metabolism includes ALL reactions — both energy-releasing (catabolism) and energy-using (anabolism). Respiration is just one part.
wrong_explanations.2: Digestion is one part of metabolism — but metabolism includes protein synthesis, DNA replication, glycogen synthesis, and many more reactions.
wrong_explanations.3: Breathing rate and movement speed are consequences of metabolic activity — metabolism itself refers to the chemical reactions, not the physical movements.

quiz.q: Why is urea produced in the liver?
opts: Excess amino acids cannot be stored — the amino group is removed (deamination) and converted to urea for excretion
Urea is produced when glucose is broken down in anaerobic respiration
The liver converts CO₂ and water into urea to reduce acid in the blood
Urea is made when fat molecules are broken down into fatty acids

wrong_explanations.1: Anaerobic respiration in animals produces LACTIC ACID — not urea. Urea comes specifically from amino acid breakdown.
wrong_explanations.2: CO₂ is transported in the blood and exhaled — it is not converted to urea.
wrong_explanations.3: Fat breakdown produces fatty acids and glycerol — not urea. Urea specifically comes from amino acid deamination.

quiz.q: Which of the following is an example of an ANABOLIC (building) reaction?
opts: Amino acids joined together at ribosomes to make a protein
Glucose broken down in aerobic respiration to release ATP
Starch digested by amylase into maltose in the mouth
Lactic acid produced in muscles during anaerobic respiration

wrong_explanations.1: Aerobic respiration BREAKS DOWN glucose — this is a catabolic reaction.
wrong_explanations.2: Digestion BREAKS DOWN starch — another catabolic reaction.
wrong_explanations.3: Lactic acid production is the result of breaking down glucose — catabolic.

foundation.common_mistake: Metabolism is the TOTAL of ALL chemical reactions — not just respiration. Students often say 'metabolism = respiration' but metabolism includes digestion, protein synthesis, DNA replication, urea production and many more reactions. Respiration is the most central metabolic process, but not the only one.

quiz.q: What is metabolism?
opts: The sum of all chemical reactions taking place in a cell or organism
Only the reactions that release energy — such as respiration
The process of digesting food in the gut
How quickly a person breathes and moves

wrong_explanations.1: Metabolism includes ALL reactions — both energy-releasing (catabolism) and energy-using (anabolism). Respiration is just one part.
wrong_explanations.2: Digestion is one part of metabolism — but metabolism includes protein synthesis, DNA replication, glycogen synthesis, and many more reactions.
wrong_explanations.3: Breathing rate and movement speed are consequences of metabolic activity — metabolism itself refers to the chemical reactions, not the physical movements.

quiz.q: Why is urea produced in the liver?
opts: Excess amino acids cannot be stored — the amino group is removed (deamination) and converted to urea for excretion
Urea is produced when glucose is broken down in anaerobic respiration
The liver converts CO₂ and water into urea to reduce acid in the blood
Urea is made when fat molecules are broken down into fatty acids

wrong_explanations.2: CO₂ is transported in the blood and exhaled — it is not converted to urea.

higher.higher_box: Metabolic rate is the rate of all chemical reactions in the body. It is affected by body size, muscle mass (metabolically active), age, temperature (in ectotherms) and hormones (thyroxine from thyroid controls basal metabolic rate). Anabolism = building molecules (protein synthesis, glycogen synthesis, lipid synthesis). Catabolism = breaking molecules down (respiration, digestion, deamination of amino acids in liver → urea).

homeostasis/homeostasis.summary: Define homeostasis and explain how negative feedback maintains a stable internal environment.

quiz.q: What is homeostasis?
opts: Maintaining a stable internal environment using negative feedback mechanisms
Increasing body temperature when the environment is hot
Removing waste products from the body through excretion
Controlling how fast organisms grow

wrong_explanations.1: Increasing body temperature when hot would make things WORSE — homeostasis CORRECTS deviations. When the body is hot, homeostasis acts to COOL it down.
wrong_explanations.2: Excretion is important but homeostasis specifically refers to maintaining STABLE internal conditions like temperature, blood glucose and water content.
wrong_explanations.3: Growth rate is controlled by hormones and genetics — not by homeostatic mechanisms.

quiz.q: Why is maintaining body temperature at 37°C so important?
opts: Enzymes in the body work best at this temperature — deviations cause slower reactions or denaturation
Bacteria cannot survive at 37°C, so the body stays infection-free
The heart muscle can only contract at exactly 37°C
Red blood cells carry oxygen most efficiently at 37°C

wrong_explanations.1: Bacteria can survive at 37°C — indeed, body temperature of ~37°C is actually ideal for many pathogens like Staphylococcus.
wrong_explanations.2: Cardiac muscle can contract across a range of temperatures — the critical issue is enzyme function at the cellular level.
wrong_explanations.3: Red blood cells do function across a range of temperatures — the enzyme sensitivity argument is the primary reason for temperature homeostasis.

quiz.q: A person's blood glucose level drops too low. What type of response will occur?
opts: Negative feedback — mechanisms are activated to raise blood glucose back to the set point
Positive feedback — more insulin is released to lower it further
No response — the body cannot detect blood glucose levels
The person's temperature rises to compensate for the energy loss

wrong_explanations.1: Positive feedback would make the problem WORSE — releasing more insulin when glucose is already low would be dangerous. Homeostasis uses negative feedback to correct deviations.
wrong_explanations.2: The pancreas and liver continuously monitor and respond to blood glucose levels — receptors in the pancreas detect low glucose and release glucagon.
wrong_explanations.3: Temperature and blood glucose are regulated independently by different systems — a drop in glucose does not trigger a temperature rise.

foundation.common_mistake: Negative feedback does NOT mean the response is negative or harmful. It means the response OPPOSES the change — it is a corrective response. If temperature rises, the negative feedback brings it back DOWN. If temperature falls, it brings it back UP. The 'negative' refers to the direction of the response relative to the change.

All homeostatic systems include: receptor (detects change), coordination centre (compares to set point, determines response) and effector (corrects deviation). Students should be able to construct and interpret diagrams of negative feedback loops.

homeostasis/nervous-system.summary: Describe the structure and function of the nervous system, neurones and synapses.

quiz.q: What is the correct pathway for a nerve signal from receptor to effector?
opts: Receptor → sensory neurone → relay neurone → motor neurone → effector
Receptor → motor neurone → relay neurone → sensory neurone → effector
Effector → sensory neurone → CNS → motor neurone → receptor
Receptor → CNS → sensory neurone → motor neurone

wrong_explanations.1: Motor and sensory neurones are in the wrong order. SENSORY carries to the CNS; MOTOR carries away from the CNS.
wrong_explanations.2: The pathway runs from receptor to effector — not in reverse.
wrong_explanations.3: Sensory neurones carry signals TO the CNS. They come AFTER the receptor, not after the CNS.

quiz.q: How does a nerve signal cross a synapse?
opts: Neurotransmitters are released, diffuse across the gap and bind to receptors on the next neurone
The electrical impulse jumps across the gap directly
The two neurones fuse temporarily to allow the signal through
Blood vessels carry chemical signals across the synaptic gap

wrong_explanations.1: The synapse is a liquid-filled gap — electrical impulses cannot jump across it. Chemical neurotransmitters are essential.
wrong_explanations.2: Neurones never physically fuse — the synaptic gap is always maintained. Fusion would prevent the one-way transmission that synapses provide.
wrong_explanations.3: Synaptic transmission takes microseconds — blood flow is far too slow. Neurotransmitters diffuse across the tiny gap.

quiz.q: Why is synaptic transmission one-directional?
opts: Neurotransmitters are released only from the pre-synaptic side, and receptors are only on the post-synaptic side
Electrical impulses can only travel in one direction through an axon
The synaptic gap is too narrow for signals to travel both ways
Enzymes on the post-synaptic side destroy neurotransmitters before they can go back

wrong_explanations.1: Electrical impulses technically can propagate in both directions in an isolated axon — the one-way nature of synapse transmission comes from the asymmetry of neurotransmitter release and receptor location.
wrong_explanations.2: Gap width has nothing to do with directionality — it's the arrangement of vesicles and receptors that enforces one-way flow.
wrong_explanations.3: Enzymes do break down neurotransmitters after binding — but this prevents prolonged stimulation, not directionality.

foundation.common_mistake: Transmission WITHIN a neurone is electrical (an impulse). Transmission ACROSS a synapse is CHEMICAL (neurotransmitters). Students often say 'electrical signals cross the synapse' — they cannot. The synapse is a gap and must be crossed using chemical neurotransmitters that then trigger a new electrical impulse in the next neurone.

opts: Receptor → sensory neurone → relay neurone → motor neurone → effector
Receptor → motor neurone → relay neurone → sensory neurone → effector
Effector → sensory neurone → CNS → motor neurone → receptor
Receptor → CNS → sensory neurone → motor neurone

Drugs affecting synapses: stimulants (caffeine, nicotine, cocaine) increase neurotransmitter activity; depressants (alcohol, benzodiazepines) reduce synaptic activity; SSRIs block serotonin reabsorption increasing its concentration. The myelin sheath insulates axons and allows faster signal conduction by saltatory (jumping) transmission.

homeostasis/reflex-actions.summary: Describe reflex actions, the reflex arc and why reflexes are faster than voluntary responses.

quiz.q: Why are reflexes faster than voluntary responses?
opts: They bypass the conscious brain — the signal is processed in the spinal cord using a shorter pathway
Reflex neurones conduct impulses faster than voluntary neurones
Reflexes use fewer neurotransmitters so synaptic delay is reduced
The brain prioritises reflex signals over other information

wrong_explanations.1: Neurone conduction speed varies by myelination — not fundamentally by whether it's a reflex or voluntary pathway.
wrong_explanations.2: Synaptic delay is similar in both — the key saving is the elimination of the brain processing step entirely.
wrong_explanations.3: The brain doesn't 'prioritise' reflexes — it is simply not involved in initiating them.

quiz.q: You touch a hot surface and pull your hand away before you feel pain. What does this demonstrate?
opts: The reflex arc bypasses the conscious brain — the withdrawal response occurred before pain was processed
Pain receptors in the hand don't work under extreme heat
The brain sends an emergency signal faster than normal in dangerous situations
Motor neurones are faster than sensory neurones

wrong_explanations.1: Pain receptors DO work — you feel the pain a fraction of a second after your hand has already moved away.
wrong_explanations.2: The brain doesn't speed up for emergencies — the reflex works independently of conscious brain processing.
wrong_explanations.3: Motor and sensory neurones have similar conduction speeds — the key is the PATHWAY length, not individual neurone speed.

quiz.q: In which part of the CNS is a spinal reflex arc processed?
opts: The spinal cord — relay neurones connect sensory and motor neurones here
The cerebral cortex — the thinking part of the brain
The cerebellum — which coordinates movement
The medulla oblongata — which controls heart rate and breathing

wrong_explanations.1: The cerebral cortex is the conscious thinking brain — reflex arcs specifically BYPASS this to be fast.
wrong_explanations.2: The cerebellum coordinates balance and smooth movement — it is not the site of spinal reflex processing.
wrong_explanations.3: The medulla oblongata controls automatic functions like heart rate — but reflex arcs for quick withdrawal responses are processed in the spinal cord.

foundation.common_mistake: A reflex bypasses the CONSCIOUS BRAIN (cerebral cortex) — it is processed in the SPINAL CORD. The brain does receive the signal (that's why you become aware of it), but it does NOT initiate the reflex. Students often say 'the brain controls reflexes' — the brain is only aware of them afterwards.

homeostasis/endocrine-system.summary: Describe the endocrine system, the main glands and hormones, and compare hormonal with nervous communication.

quiz.q: How do hormones reach their target organs?
opts: Via the bloodstream — secreted directly into the blood by endocrine glands
Via nerve fibres — along the same pathways as electrical impulses
Through ducts that lead directly to the target organ
They diffuse through tissue fluid between neighbouring cells only

wrong_explanations.1: Hormones travel in BLOOD, not along nerves — this is the fundamental difference between the two communication systems.
wrong_explanations.2: EXOCRINE glands (e.g. salivary glands, sweat glands) use ducts. ENDOCRINE glands secrete directly into the blood — they have no ducts.
wrong_explanations.3: Hormones travel long distances in the blood — they don't diffuse locally like paracrine signals.

quiz.q: Which hormone prepares the body for 'fight or flight'?
opts: Adrenaline — released by the adrenal glands in response to stress or danger
Insulin — released by the pancreas to lower blood glucose
Thyroxine — released by the thyroid to increase metabolic rate
Oestrogen — released by the ovaries to prepare the body for reproduction

wrong_explanations.1: Insulin lowers blood glucose — it is released after eating, not in response to danger.
wrong_explanations.2: Thyroxine controls long-term metabolic rate — it is not the emergency stress hormone.
wrong_explanations.3: Oestrogen is involved in the menstrual cycle — not in acute stress responses.

quiz.q: Which best describes the difference between nervous and hormonal communication?
opts: Nervous = fast, short-lived, specific target via nerves. Hormonal = slower, longer-lasting, via bloodstream to target organs.
Nervous = slow, long-lasting. Hormonal = fast, short-lived.
They work in the same way — both use chemicals to transmit signals.
Nervous system only controls muscles. Hormonal system only controls glands.

wrong_explanations.1: This is exactly the wrong way round. Nervous is FAST and SHORT. Hormonal is SLOWER and LONGER-LASTING.
wrong_explanations.2: Both do use chemicals at some point (neurotransmitters at synapses / hormones in blood) — but their overall mechanisms, speeds and targets are very different.
wrong_explanations.3: The nervous system controls both muscles AND glands. Hormones affect many different tissues — not just glands.

foundation.common_mistake: Hormones travel in the BLOOD to target organs — they do NOT travel along nerves. Do not confuse the two systems. Also: not all organs respond to every hormone — only TARGET ORGANS with the correct receptor proteins respond.

homeostasis/blood-glucose-diabetes.summary: Describe how insulin and glucagon regulate blood glucose, and explain Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes.

quiz.q: Blood glucose rises after eating. Which hormone is released and what does it do?
opts: Insulin — released by pancreas beta cells, causes glucose to be converted to glycogen in the liver
Glucagon — released by pancreas alpha cells, causes glycogen to be broken down
Adrenaline — released by adrenal glands, increases blood glucose further
Thyroxine — released by thyroid, converts glucose to glycogen

wrong_explanations.1: Glucagon is released when blood glucose is TOO LOW — it would make a post-meal rise even worse.
wrong_explanations.2: Adrenaline does raise blood glucose (for fight or flight) — but is not the routine homeostatic response to eating.
wrong_explanations.3: Thyroxine controls metabolic rate — it does not regulate blood glucose directly.

quiz.q: Why must Type 1 diabetics inject insulin rather than take it as a tablet?
opts: Insulin is a protein — it would be digested and destroyed by proteases in the gut before reaching the blood
Insulin tablets haven't been invented yet
The stomach acid destroys insulin too slowly for it to work
Tablets cannot reach the pancreas where insulin needs to work

wrong_explanations.1: Oral insulin formulations are being researched (with special protective coatings) but the traditional reason is protein digestion in the gut.
wrong_explanations.2: It's not about speed — insulin is a large protein molecule that gets broken down into amino acids by digestive enzymes before being absorbed.
wrong_explanations.3: Insulin doesn't work IN the pancreas — it is secreted by the pancreas and works on LIVER and MUSCLE cells. The issue is gut digestion.

quiz.q: What is the key difference between Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes?
opts: Type 1: pancreas produces no insulin (autoimmune). Type 2: cells become resistant to insulin (lifestyle-related).
Type 1 is more common than Type 2
Type 2 requires insulin injections; Type 1 can be managed with diet alone
Both types are caused by the immune system destroying pancreas cells

wrong_explanations.1: Type 2 is FAR MORE COMMON — it makes up about 90% of all diabetes cases. Type 1 is less common.
wrong_explanations.2: This is the wrong way round. TYPE 1 requires insulin injections because the pancreas cannot make it. TYPE 2 is usually managed first with diet, exercise and tablets.
wrong_explanations.3: Only TYPE 1 is autoimmune. TYPE 2 develops due to insulin resistance — the immune system is not the cause.

foundation.common_mistake: INSULIN lowers blood glucose by promoting GLYCOGEN STORAGE (glucose → glycogen). GLUCAGON raises blood glucose by promoting GLYCOGEN BREAKDOWN (glycogen → glucose). Students constantly confuse which hormone does which. Memory trick: INsulin = INto storage. Glucagon = Get glucose out of storage.

fifas.label: Blood Glucose Negative Feedback
fifas.question: Describe what happens to blood glucose and hormones after a person eats a meal containing a large amount of carbohydrates.
steps: Identify the change: carbohydrates digested → glucose absorbed → blood glucose RISES
Pancreas beta cells detect the rise → release INSULIN into the blood
Insulin travels to liver and muscles → glucose converted to glycogen (glycogenesis)
Blood glucose FALLS back to ~5 mmol/L — negative feedback complete

higher.higher_box: Insulin (from pancreatic beta cells) causes glucose uptake and glycogenesis (glucose → glycogen). Glucagon (from alpha cells) causes glycogenolysis (glycogen → glucose) and gluconeogenesis (making new glucose). Negative feedback maintains blood glucose at ~4–6 mmol/L. Type 2: insulin resistance — managed with diet, exercise, metformin. Both lead to hyperglycaemia if untreated.

homeostasis/human-reproduction-hormones.summary: Describe puberty, the menstrual cycle and the hormones that control human reproduction.

quiz.q: Which hormone triggers ovulation?
opts: LH (Luteinising Hormone) — a surge at around Day 14 releases the egg from the follicle
FSH — it stimulates the follicle to release the egg
Oestrogen — high oestrogen directly causes egg release
Progesterone — it relaxes the follicle wall to release the egg

wrong_explanations.1: FSH stimulates EGG MATURATION — it prepares the follicle but doesn't trigger the release. The LH SURGE causes ovulation.
wrong_explanations.2: High oestrogen causes the RELEASE OF LH from the pituitary — it's the subsequent LH surge that triggers ovulation, not oestrogen directly.
wrong_explanations.3: Progesterone is produced AFTER ovulation by the corpus luteum — it maintains the uterus lining. It does not trigger ovulation.

quiz.q: What happens to progesterone levels if fertilisation does NOT occur?
opts: Progesterone falls — the uterus lining breaks down — menstruation begins and the cycle restarts
Progesterone rises — signalling the body to prepare for pregnancy anyway
Progesterone stays the same indefinitely until the next menstrual cycle
Progesterone triggers FSH release to immediately start a new cycle

wrong_explanations.1: Progesterone rising would signal PREGNANCY — which hasn't occurred. Without an embryo producing hormones, the corpus luteum degenerates and progesterone falls.
wrong_explanations.2: Progesterone cannot stay elevated indefinitely — the corpus luteum degenerates after about 14 days without an embryo implanting.
wrong_explanations.3: FSH is released at the start of a new cycle as progesterone falls — but it is oestrogen and progesterone levels, not progesterone directly stimulating FSH.

quiz.q: What is the role of oestrogen in the menstrual cycle?
opts: Repairs and thickens the uterus lining after menstruation — also triggers the LH surge at high levels
Triggers ovulation directly at low concentrations
Maintains the uterus lining in the second half of the cycle
Stimulates egg maturation in the follicle

wrong_explanations.1: Oestrogen at HIGH concentrations triggers LH release, and LH causes ovulation — but at LOW concentrations, oestrogen inhibits FSH, not triggers ovulation.
wrong_explanations.2: Maintaining the uterus lining in the second half = PROGESTERONE. Oestrogen rebuilds it in the first half.
wrong_explanations.3: Stimulating egg maturation = FSH. Oestrogen is produced AS A RESULT of FSH stimulation, not a cause of egg maturation.

foundation.common_mistake: FSH stimulates EGG MATURATION (not ovulation). LH triggers OVULATION. Oestrogen REBUILDS the uterus lining. Progesterone MAINTAINS the lining. The pituitary produces FSH and LH. The ovaries produce oestrogen and progesterone. Students often confuse which gland makes which hormone.

homeostasis/contraception-fertility.summary: Describe methods of contraception and fertility treatments including IVF.

quiz.q: Which contraceptive method ALSO protects against sexually transmitted infections?
opts: Condoms (male or female) — barrier methods prevent both pregnancy and STI transmission
The combined contraceptive pill
The copper IUD
The contraceptive implant

wrong_explanations.1: The pill prevents pregnancy hormonally but does not prevent STI transmission — pathogens can still be exchanged.
wrong_explanations.2: The copper IUD works inside the uterus — it doesn't prevent STI transmission during sexual contact.
wrong_explanations.3: The implant prevents ovulation — it provides no barrier against pathogen transmission.

quiz.q: In IVF, why is FSH given to the woman at the start of treatment?
opts: To stimulate the ovaries to produce multiple eggs so more can be collected and fertilised
To prepare the uterus lining for embryo implantation
To prevent the immune system from rejecting the embryo
To fertilise the eggs in the laboratory

wrong_explanations.1: Preparing the uterus lining for implantation = PROGESTERONE — given later in IVF treatment. FSH specifically stimulates egg production.
wrong_explanations.2: Immune suppression is managed separately — FSH is specifically a reproductive hormone that drives follicle development and egg production.
wrong_explanations.3: Fertilisation is done by adding sperm to eggs in a laboratory dish — FSH is a hormone, not a fertilisation agent.

quiz.q: How does the combined contraceptive pill prevent pregnancy?
opts: The oestrogen it contains inhibits FSH production — no FSH means no egg maturation and no ovulation
It thickens the cervical mucus so sperm cannot swim through
It prevents sperm from entering the vagina during sex
It causes the uterus lining to shed more frequently

wrong_explanations.1: Thickening cervical mucus = PROGESTERONE-ONLY PILL (mini-pill). The COMBINED pill works primarily by suppressing ovulation through oestrogen inhibiting FSH.
wrong_explanations.2: Preventing sperm entering = BARRIER METHODS (condoms, diaphragm). The pill has no physical barrier effect.
wrong_explanations.3: The combined pill actually STABILISES the uterus lining — frequent shedding would be a side effect of other hormone imbalances, not the pill's mechanism.

foundation.common_mistake: The contraceptive pill does NOT protect against STIs — only BARRIER METHODS (condoms) protect against both pregnancy AND sexually transmitted infections. The combined pill prevents OVULATION by inhibiting FSH — without ovulation, there is no egg to fertilise. In IVF, FSH is given to stimulate MULTIPLE egg production — not to help with implantation (progesterone does that).

Ethical issues: disposal of unused embryos, risk of multiple births, access and cost, use of donor eggs/sperm. Students should be able to evaluate these issues from multiple perspectives.

homeostasis/reaction-time.summary: Describe how to measure reaction time and explain what affects it.

quiz.q: In a ruler drop test, a ruler falls 45 cm before being caught. Which calculation gives the reaction time? (g = 10 m/s²)

wrong_explanations.1: 45 cm must be converted to metres (0.45 m) before using in the formula. Using 45 gives a nonsensically large answer.
wrong_explanations.2: t = d/g is not the correct formula. The correct rearrangement of d = ½gt² gives t = √(2d/g).
wrong_explanations.3: Multiplying gives a large number — the formula requires square root of (2d/g), not multiplication of all terms.

quiz.q: Why should a reaction time experiment use multiple repeats and calculate the mean?
opts: Reaction time varies randomly between trials — the mean gives a more reliable estimate of true reaction time
The ruler wears out and becomes less accurate after each drop
The person's brain learns the formula and gets better at calculating the answer
Multiple repeats prevent the person from practising and improving

wrong_explanations.1: Rulers don't wear out with use — the variation is in the human nervous system response, not the equipment.
wrong_explanations.2: The person does not calculate anything during the experiment — they simply catch the ruler. Multiple repeats address random biological variation.
wrong_explanations.3: Practice effects are a potential confound — but the PRIMARY reason for multiple repeats is to reduce random variation in measurements.

foundation.common_mistake: When calculating reaction time from the ruler drop test — distance must be in METRES (not cm). g = 10 m/s² (or 9.8 m/s² for more precision). Rearrange d = ½gt² to get t = √(2d/g). Always take multiple repeats and use the MEAN — single measurements are unreliable.

fifas.label: Reaction Time Calculation
fifas.question: A ruler falls 20 cm before being caught. Calculate the reaction time. (g = 10 m/s²)

inheritance/sexual-asexual-reproduction.summary: Compare sexual and asexual reproduction and explain the role of meiosis in sexual reproduction.

quiz.q: What is the main genetic advantage of sexual reproduction over asexual reproduction?
opts: It creates genetic variation — offspring differ from parents, allowing adaptation to changing environments
It is faster — more offspring are produced in less time
Only one parent is needed — saving time and energy
All offspring are perfectly adapted — no risk of poor survival

wrong_explanations.1: ASEXUAL reproduction is faster — producing large numbers of identical offspring quickly. Sexual reproduction is slower but creates variation.
wrong_explanations.2: Requiring only ONE parent = ASEXUAL reproduction. Sexual reproduction needs two parents.
wrong_explanations.3: It is ASEXUAL reproduction where all offspring are identical clones of a well-adapted parent. Sexual reproduction means offspring vary.

quiz.q: Why does meiosis halve the chromosome number?
opts: So that when two gametes fuse at fertilisation, the offspring has the correct number (46) and not double (92)
To create more genetic variation by reducing the number of genes
Because cells with fewer chromosomes divide more easily
Meiosis does not halve chromosome number — it doubles it

wrong_explanations.1: Fewer chromosomes do allow more combinations, but the PRIMARY reason for halving is to prevent the chromosome number doubling each generation.
wrong_explanations.2: Cell division speed is not the biological reason — the function is specifically to allow fertilisation to restore the correct diploid number.
wrong_explanations.3: Meiosis HALVES chromosome number (46 → 23). Doubling occurs at fertilisation when two haploid gametes combine to make a diploid zygote.

quiz.q: Bacteria reproduce by binary fission — splitting into two identical cells. What type of reproduction is this?
opts: Asexual reproduction — one parent, offspring are genetically identical clones
Sexual reproduction — two bacteria combine to share genetic material
Meiosis — the chromosome number is halved in each new bacterium
Fertilisation — two gametes fuse to form a new bacterium

wrong_explanations.1: Some bacteria do exchange genetic material (conjugation) — but binary fission specifically produces identical copies from one parent. It is asexual.
wrong_explanations.2: Binary fission produces identical cells from ONE parent — it does not involve halving chromosome numbers. Bacteria are haploid anyway.
wrong_explanations.3: Gametes are specialised sex cells — bacteria don't produce gametes in binary fission.

foundation.common_mistake: MITOSIS produces IDENTICAL cells (used for growth and repair). MEIOSIS produces GAMETES with HALF the chromosome number — and they are genetically different from each other. Students often confuse these two. Remember: MEiosis → gamEtes. Mitosis → most body cells.

Independent assortment (random orientation of chromosome pairs) and crossing over both increase genetic variation. The combination of meiosis and fertilisation produces enormous genetic diversity.

inheritance/dna-genome.summary: Describe the structure of DNA, what genes are, and what the human genome project achieved.

quiz.q: In DNA, which bases pair together?
opts: A with T, and C with G
A with G, and C with T
A with C, and G with T
All four bases pair randomly

wrong_explanations.1: Wrong pairing — A pairs with T (2 hydrogen bonds) and C pairs with G (3 hydrogen bonds). A-G pairings do not occur.
wrong_explanations.2: Wrong pairing — A-C and G-T pairings do not occur in DNA. Base pairing is strictly A-T and C-G.
wrong_explanations.3: Base pairing in DNA is NOT random — it is strictly determined by molecular structure. A-T and C-G only.

quiz.q: What is a gene?
opts: A specific sequence of DNA bases that codes for a particular protein
A complete chromosome containing all the genetic information
A unit of heredity that controls physical appearance only
Any section of DNA — all DNA constitutes genes

wrong_explanations.1: A complete chromosome contains thousands of genes — it is not itself a gene.
wrong_explanations.2: Genes code for proteins, which then influence characteristics — but genes control far more than physical appearance (e.g. enzyme production, cell signalling).
wrong_explanations.3: Not all DNA codes for proteins — a significant portion of the genome is non-coding DNA. Genes are specifically the protein-coding sequences.

quiz.q: What was the Human Genome Project?
opts: An international project that sequenced all ~3 billion base pairs of human DNA and mapped all human genes
A project to create genetically modified humans with improved characteristics
A database of all known genetic diseases and their cures
A study comparing the DNA of humans to chimpanzees

wrong_explanations.1: The Human Genome Project was about SEQUENCING and MAPPING the genome — not about genetically modifying humans.
wrong_explanations.2: While comparisons with other species were made using genome data, the primary goal was mapping and sequencing the HUMAN genome itself.
wrong_explanations.3: While the HGP has helped identify disease genes, it was a mapping and sequencing project — not a medical treatment database.

foundation.common_mistake: A GENE is not the same as a CHROMOSOME. A chromosome contains THOUSANDS of genes. The GENOME is the COMPLETE set of ALL genetic information — all genes on all chromosomes. Base pairing: A pairs with T only. C pairs with G only. Never A-G or C-T.

The Human Genome Project identified ~20,000 protein-coding genes out of ~3 billion base pairs — most DNA is non-coding.

inheritance/genetic-inheritance.summary: Explain dominant and recessive inheritance and use Punnett squares to predict offspring ratios.

quiz.q: In a Bb × Bb cross, what fraction of offspring will show the RECESSIVE phenotype?
opts: 0 — recessive cannot appear from two heterozygous parents

wrong_explanations.1: 50% occurs when one parent is homozygous recessive (bb × Bb). Two heterozygotes give 1 BB : 2 Bb : 1 bb → 25% recessive.
wrong_explanations.2: 3/4 is the proportion showing the DOMINANT phenotype (BB and Bb). Only 25% (bb) show recessive.
wrong_explanations.3: Two heterozygous parents (Bb × Bb) absolutely CAN produce a recessive child — 25% chance (bb).

quiz.q: A person is described as a 'carrier' of cystic fibrosis. What does this mean?
opts: They have one recessive allele (Ff) — they appear healthy but can pass the allele to offspring
They have the disease mildly — they have some symptoms but not severe ones
They are homozygous recessive (ff) — they have the full condition
They have been exposed to cystic fibrosis but their immune system fought it off

wrong_explanations.1: A carrier appears COMPLETELY NORMAL — there are no mild symptoms. Having one recessive allele (Ff) is masked by the dominant allele.
wrong_explanations.2: Homozygous recessive (ff) = HAS the condition. A carrier is HETEROZYGOUS (Ff) and does NOT have the condition.
wrong_explanations.3: Cystic fibrosis is a GENETIC condition — it is not an infection that can be 'fought off' by the immune system.

quiz.q: If a Punnett square shows a 1 in 4 chance of having an affected child, what does this mean for a couple?
opts: Each pregnancy independently has a 25% chance of being affected — it does not mean exactly 1 in 4 children will be affected
Exactly one child out of every four they have will be affected
If their first three children are unaffected, the fourth will definitely be affected
The chance reduces after each unaffected child — the probability changes

wrong_explanations.1: Probability is not a guarantee of exact outcomes — with only 4 children, you might have 0, 1, 2, 3 or even 4 affected children.
wrong_explanations.2: Each pregnancy is INDEPENDENT — the outcome of previous pregnancies does not affect future ones. This is like flipping a coin.
wrong_explanations.3: Probability doesn't 'accumulate' — each pregnancy always has the same 25% chance regardless of previous outcomes.

foundation.common_mistake: A CARRIER (Bb) has the recessive allele but does NOT show the condition — they appear completely normal. Students often say carriers 'have the disease mildly' — this is WRONG. A carrier is phenotypically normal but genotypically heterozygous. Two carriers (Bb × Bb) have a 25% chance of an affected (bb) child.

fifas.label: Punnett Square — Heterozygous Cross
fifas.question: Two parents are both heterozygous for brown eyes (Bb). Brown (B) is dominant over blue (b). Calculate the probability of a child having blue eyes.
steps: Draw a Punnett square: Bb × Bb
Offspring: BB, Bb, Bb, bb → genotype ratio 1:2:1
Only bb gives blue eyes (recessive phenotype) = 1 out of 4

higher.higher_box: Codominance: both alleles expressed in the phenotype. Example: blood groups — IA and IB are codominant; IO is recessive. Blood group AB genotype = IAIB (both A and B antigens expressed). Students should be able to construct Punnett squares for crosses involving codominant alleles and predict the phenotype ratios for codominant inheritance.

inheritance/inherited-disorders.summary: Describe cystic fibrosis and polydactyly as examples of inherited genetic disorders.

quiz.q: Both parents appear healthy but have a child with cystic fibrosis. What must be true of the parents?
opts: Both parents are carriers — genotype Ff — they have one copy of the recessive allele each
One parent must have cystic fibrosis but is hiding it
The child developed CF through a random environmental mutation
The parents must be closely related — CF only occurs in related families

wrong_explanations.1: CF is a genetic condition — you cannot 'hide' it. If a parent had CF (ff) they would have serious symptoms.
wrong_explanations.2: CF is caused by inheriting TWO recessive alleles — both alleles must come from parents. The parents must both be carriers (Ff).
wrong_explanations.3: While CF is more common in some populations, it is not caused by family relatedness — it requires two carriers regardless of family relationship.

quiz.q: Why does polydactyly appear in every generation, while cystic fibrosis can skip generations?
opts: Polydactyly is dominant — one copy is enough to show it. CF is recessive — carriers (Ff) appear normal and don't show it.
Polydactyly is caused by a different chromosome than cystic fibrosis
Cystic fibrosis is more severe so the body suppresses it in some generations
Polydactyly affects more people so it appears more often

wrong_explanations.1: The chromosomal location of a gene is irrelevant to whether the condition skips generations — it's the dominant/recessive nature that matters.
wrong_explanations.2: The body doesn't suppress genetic conditions — whether a condition shows depends entirely on genotype and allele dominance.
wrong_explanations.3: Prevalence doesn't determine whether a condition skips generations — dominance/recessiveness does.

foundation.common_mistake: Cystic fibrosis is RECESSIVE — both parents can be unaffected carriers. Polydactyly is DOMINANT — at least one parent will always be affected (unless it arose from a new mutation). Students often apply these rules the wrong way round. Remember: recessive conditions can SKIP GENERATIONS (via carriers). Dominant conditions CANNOT skip generations.

fifas.question: Two carriers for cystic fibrosis (Ff × Ff) are expecting a child. What is the probability the child will have CF?
steps: Draw Punnett square: Ff × Ff. CF allele = f (recessive). Normal = F
Offspring: FF, Ff, Ff, ff → ratio 1:2:1
Only ff has cystic fibrosis = 1 out of 4 boxes

higher.higher_box: Embryo screening (PGD — preimplantation genetic diagnosis): embryos from IVF can be tested for genetic conditions before implantation. Allows selection of unaffected embryos. Ethical issues: is this a slippery slope to designer babies? Does it devalue people with that condition? Genetic counselling helps families understand inheritance patterns and risks without being directive about choices.

inheritance/sex-determination.summary: Explain how biological sex is determined by the X and Y chromosomes.

quiz.q: What determines the biological sex of a human offspring?
opts: Which type of sperm (X or Y) fertilises the egg — Y-sperm → male, X-sperm → female
The egg — females produce X and Y eggs, one type producing a boy and the other a girl
The environment during pregnancy — temperature affects sex
The age of the parents — older parents are more likely to have girls

wrong_explanations.1: Females (XX) can only produce X-bearing eggs — all eggs contain X. It is the SPERM that carries either X or Y.
wrong_explanations.2: Temperature does affect sex determination in some reptiles — but in humans, sex is determined genetically by which sperm fertilises the egg.
wrong_explanations.3: Parental age can affect fertility and some genetic risks — but does not determine sex.

quiz.q: A couple have three daughters. What is the probability their next child will be a son?
opts: 50% — each pregnancy is independent, the sex ratio is always 50:50
Higher than 50% — after three girls they are 'due' a boy
Lower than 50% — this couple clearly produce more girls than boys
0% — if they have had three girls their sperm cannot produce Y chromosomes

wrong_explanations.1: Each pregnancy is INDEPENDENT — like flipping a coin, the outcomes of previous pregnancies have no effect. This is a common misconception known as the gambler's fallacy.
wrong_explanations.2: Having three girls does not indicate a bias — small sample sizes often show runs of one outcome by chance. Males produce X and Y sperm equally.
wrong_explanations.3: Males always produce roughly 50% X-bearing and 50% Y-bearing sperm — three daughters doesn't change this.

foundation.common_mistake: It is the SPERM that determines the sex of the child — not the egg. All eggs contain an X chromosome. Sperm can contain either X or Y. If a Y-carrying sperm fertilises the egg, the child is male (XY). Historically, women were sometimes blamed for not producing sons — this is biologically incorrect.

quiz.q: What determines the biological sex of a human offspring?
opts: Which type of sperm (X or Y) fertilises the egg — Y-sperm → male, X-sperm → female

Males (XY) have only one X — so any allele on their single X is expressed, even if recessive. This is why X-linked recessive conditions (colour blindness, haemophilia) are more common in males. Females can be carriers — one recessive allele masked by the dominant on the second X. Students should be able to construct and interpret Punnett squares for X-linked inheritance.

inheritance/variation.summary: Describe the causes of variation and distinguish between genetic, environmental and combination variation.

quiz.q: A child is taller than expected given their parents' heights. What is the most likely cause?
opts: A combination of genetic potential and favourable environmental factors — e.g. excellent nutrition during childhood
A mutation that occurred during development, making the child taller
The child inherited more height genes from distant ancestors than from parents
Environmental factors alone — height is entirely determined by diet

wrong_explanations.1: A random mutation could theoretically increase height, but this is rare. The more likely explanation is that parents didn't reach their own genetic potential due to environmental factors.
wrong_explanations.2: Genes don't skip generations in this simple way — the child can only inherit alleles from their parents.
wrong_explanations.3: Height is strongly influenced by genetics — children of tall parents tend to be taller. But environmental factors (particularly nutrition) also play a significant role.

quiz.q: Which of the following is an example of DISCONTINUOUS variation?
opts: Blood group — a person is type A, B, AB or O with no intermediate values
Height — people range from very short to very tall with all values in between
Body weight — a continuous range from very light to very heavy
Skin colour — a continuous range from very light to very dark

wrong_explanations.1: Height is a classic example of CONTINUOUS variation — there is a smooth range from shortest to tallest with all intermediate heights present.
wrong_explanations.2: Body weight is CONTINUOUS — it forms a normal distribution curve across the population.
wrong_explanations.3: Skin colour is CONTINUOUS — determined by multiple genes and environmental factors (sun exposure), producing a smooth range of values.

quiz.q: What is a mutation?
opts: A change in the sequence of DNA bases — can be neutral, harmful or rarely beneficial
A change in an organism's body caused by the environment during its lifetime
The process of chromosomes being shuffled during meiosis
A genetic disease inherited from parents

wrong_explanations.1: Environmental changes to the body (like a tan or muscle growth) are not mutations — they are phenotypic responses that are not passed on through DNA.
wrong_explanations.2: Chromosome shuffling during meiosis = INDEPENDENT ASSORTMENT and CROSSING OVER — these create variation but are not mutations.
wrong_explanations.3: Inherited diseases are caused by specific alleles — mutations can create new alleles, but the inherited disease itself is not a mutation.

foundation.common_mistake: Environmental variation is NOT inherited — you cannot pass your experiences, injuries or learned skills to your children through DNA. Genetic variation IS inherited. Most mutations are neutral — not harmful. Students often assume all mutations are dangerous, but the vast majority have no detectable effect.
