Students should understand that natural selection acts on existing variation — it cannot produce new alleles on demand.

inheritance/evolution-natural-selection.summary: Describe Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection and explain how populations change over time.

quiz.q: A population of mice lives on dark soil. Over many generations, the population becomes predominantly dark-coloured. What explains this?
opts: Natural selection — dark mice are better camouflaged, survive predation better and reproduce more, passing on dark colour alleles
The mice deliberately changed colour to match their environment
Dark soil contains minerals that cause mice to become darker
Dark-coloured mice migrated from elsewhere and replaced the lighter mice

wrong_explanations.1: Animals cannot deliberately change their genetic traits — they cannot 'choose' to become better adapted.
wrong_explanations.2: Environmental exposure doesn't directly change DNA — this would be Lamarckian evolution, which has been disproved. Colour is a genetic trait, not caused by soil minerals.
wrong_explanations.3: While migration is possible, natural selection is the primary explanation — lighter mice are more visible to predators and are caught more often.

quiz.q: Why was Darwin's theory of natural selection initially controversial?
opts: It contradicted religious beliefs about creation, and Darwin lacked knowledge of the mechanism of inheritance
It was based on observations from only one location and no experiments were done
Darwin published it before collecting sufficient evidence, making it speculative
The theory was too simple — scientists thought evolution must be more complicated

wrong_explanations.1: Darwin travelled extensively (including the Galapagos, South America, Australia) and collected extensive evidence over many years.
wrong_explanations.2: Darwin actually delayed publication for over 20 years while gathering more evidence — he published when he felt the evidence was compelling.
wrong_explanations.3: The theory was considered TOO radical and COMPLICATED for many — particularly the idea of species changing over millions of years.

quiz.q: What does 'survival of the fittest' mean in evolution?
opts: Individuals best adapted to their environment are most likely to survive and reproduce — not necessarily the strongest
The physically strongest and fastest individuals always survive
Only the largest organisms in a population will reproduce
Individuals that are most intelligent always out-compete others

wrong_explanations.1: Strength is one possible advantage in some environments — but 'fittest' specifically means best suited to the environment. A slow, toxic frog is 'fitter' than a fast but tasty one in a predator-rich environment.
wrong_explanations.2: Size can be a disadvantage — larger organisms often need more food and may be more visible to predators.
wrong_explanations.3: Intelligence is advantageous in some contexts — but many highly successful organisms (bacteria, insects) have no intelligence.

foundation.common_mistake: Natural selection does NOT cause mutations — mutations happen randomly. Natural selection SELECTS from variation that already exists. Also: individuals do NOT evolve — POPULATIONS evolve over many generations. And: 'survival of the fittest' does not mean 'strongest' — it means best adapted to the environment.

higher.higher_box: Speciation: geographic isolation divides a population → different selection pressures → allele frequencies diverge → accumulation of genetic differences → reproductive isolation → no longer able to interbreed → two species. Alfred Russel Wallace co-developed the theory of natural selection independently. Modern classification uses DNA sequence comparisons and evolutionary relationships (phylogenetics) rather than physical characteristics alone.

inheritance/selective-breeding.summary: Describe how selective breeding works and its applications and disadvantages.

quiz.q: What is the main disadvantage of selective breeding over many generations?
opts: Reduced genetic diversity — the gene pool narrows, making populations vulnerable to new diseases or environmental changes
Selective breeding always produces weaker offspring than wild populations
It produces organisms that cannot survive outside captivity or cultivation
The desired traits always disappear after a few generations

wrong_explanations.1: Selectively bred organisms are often very productive — but reduced genetic diversity is the key long-term risk.
wrong_explanations.2: Many selectively bred organisms can survive in natural conditions — though some extreme examples (like bulldogs) do have welfare issues.
wrong_explanations.3: Selective breeding fixes desired traits into the genome — they don't disappear unless the selection pressure is removed and interbreeding with different varieties occurs.

quiz.q: How is selective breeding different from genetic engineering?
opts: Selective breeding uses natural reproduction to concentrate existing alleles. Genetic engineering directly inserts, modifies or removes specific genes.
Selective breeding is faster than genetic engineering
Selective breeding creates entirely new genes. Genetic engineering uses existing ones.
They are essentially the same process — both involve deliberately changing an organism's DNA

wrong_explanations.1: Selective breeding is SLOW — it takes many generations. Genetic engineering can produce results in one generation.
wrong_explanations.2: Selective breeding works with EXISTING genetic variation — it cannot create new genes. Genetic engineering can introduce genes from completely different species.
wrong_explanations.3: Selective breeding does NOT directly change DNA sequences — it selects which individuals reproduce. Genetic engineering directly manipulates DNA.

foundation.common_mistake: Selective breeding is NOT genetic engineering. Selective breeding uses natural reproduction — choosing which organisms breed. Genetic engineering directly inserts, removes or modifies specific genes. Selective breeding also does NOT create new genes — it selects and concentrates alleles that already exist in the population.

Students should be able to evaluate the ethical implications of selective breeding including animal welfare concerns.

inheritance/genetic-engineering.summary: Describe genetic engineering — how it works, examples and ethical considerations.

quiz.q: Why is GM bacterial insulin better for diabetics than insulin extracted from pigs or cattle?
opts: GM bacterial insulin is identical to human insulin — fewer immune reactions and side effects
Bacterial insulin is cheaper to produce so more people can afford it
Animal-derived insulin is no longer available — GM insulin is the only option
GM bacteria produce insulin faster so it acts more quickly in the body

wrong_explanations.1: Cost is an advantage, but the primary medical reason is that human sequence insulin causes fewer immune problems than animal sequence insulin.
wrong_explanations.2: Animal-derived insulin still exists in some countries — GM insulin has largely replaced it due to superior compatibility.
wrong_explanations.3: The speed of action is determined by the formulation (how the insulin is prepared), not where it came from.

quiz.q: What is the basic process of introducing a new gene into an organism?
opts: Cut the desired gene using restriction enzymes → insert into a plasmid vector → introduce the vector into the target cell
Inject the desired gene directly into the cell nucleus using a syringe
Breed the organism with another species that carries the desired gene
Expose the organism to radiation to trigger the mutation needed

wrong_explanations.1: While microinjection into cells is a real technique used in some contexts, the standard recombinant DNA approach uses restriction enzymes and plasmid vectors.
wrong_explanations.2: Breeding between different species = selective breeding (and only works between related species). Genetic engineering specifically allows cross-species gene transfer.
wrong_explanations.3: Radiation causes RANDOM mutations — not the specific targeted change needed for genetic engineering.

foundation.common_mistake: Genetic engineering is NOT the same as selective breeding. Genetic engineering can transfer genes between completely different species (e.g. human genes into bacteria). Selective breeding can only work with organisms that can naturally reproduce together. Also: eating GM food does not alter the consumer's own DNA.

higher.higher_box: Restriction enzymes cut DNA at specific recognition sequences. Ligase enzymes join DNA fragments. The target gene is inserted into a vector (usually a bacterial plasmid). The vector is introduced into the host cell (bacteria, plant, animal). GM bacteria produce human insulin (insulin gene from human DNA inserted into E. coli). Students should evaluate both the potential benefits (medicine, food security) and risks (gene escape, biodiversity reduction, corporate control of food supply) of GM organisms.

inheritance/evidence-for-evolution.summary: Describe the main types of evidence that support the theory of evolution.

quiz.q: Why do the pentadactyl (five-digit) limbs of humans, whales and bats support evolution?
opts: They share the same underlying bone structure despite very different functions — suggesting a common ancestor whose limb adapted differently in each lineage
They show that all vertebrates use their limbs in the same way — a universal design
Five-digit limbs are the most efficient design, so evolution always produces them independently
It shows that whales were once land animals that walked on five-toed feet

wrong_explanations.1: Vertebrate limbs are used very differently — for flying, swimming, running and grasping. The similarity in structure, despite different functions, is precisely what makes it evidence of common ancestry.
wrong_explanations.2: If five digits were universally optimal, we'd expect to see it in insects and fish too — but they don't have this arrangement. The pattern is phylogenetic (family-tree based), not design-based.
wrong_explanations.3: While it's true whales evolved from land mammals, the pentadactyl limb evidence is specifically about STRUCTURAL SIMILARITY across multiple species, not specifically about whale evolution.

quiz.q: Why does antibiotic resistance in bacteria provide evidence for evolution?
opts: It demonstrates natural selection occurring in real time — resistant variants survive and reproduce, changing the population
It shows that bacteria can deliberately change their DNA in response to threats
It proves that antibiotics cause mutations in bacteria
It shows that evolution only happens in very simple organisms like bacteria

wrong_explanations.1: Bacteria cannot choose or deliberately change their DNA — resistance arises from RANDOM mutations that existed before antibiotic exposure.
wrong_explanations.2: Antibiotics SELECT resistant variants — they don't cause the mutations. The mutations existed before the antibiotic was used.
wrong_explanations.3: Evolution occurs in all organisms — bacteria just evolve faster because they reproduce rapidly (millions of generations in days). Natural selection has been directly observed in larger organisms too.

foundation.common_mistake: The fossil record is INCOMPLETE — most organisms never fossilise. This means gaps in the fossil record are EXPECTED and do not disprove evolution. Students often say 'if there are gaps in the fossil record, evolution can't be proven' — but the overall pattern of fossils strongly supports evolution, even with gaps.

It shows that whales were once land animals that walked on five-toed feet

wrong_explanations.1: Vertebrate limbs are used very differently — for flying, swimming, running and grasping. The similarity in structure, despite different functions, is precisely what makes it evidence of common ancestry.
wrong_explanations.2: If five digits were universally optimal, we'd expect to see it in insects and fish too — but they don't have this arrangement. The pattern is phylogenetic (family-tree based), not design-based.
wrong_explanations.3: While it's true whales evolved from land mammals, the pentadactyl limb evidence is specifically about STRUCTURAL SIMILARITY across multiple species, not specifically about whale evolution.

quiz.q: Why does antibiotic resistance in bacteria provide evidence for evolution?
opts: It demonstrates natural selection occurring in real time — resistant variants survive and reproduce, changing the population
It shows that bacteria can deliberately change their DNA in response to threats
It proves that antibiotics cause mutations in bacteria
It shows that evolution only happens in very simple organisms like bacteria

wrong_explanations.1: Bacteria cannot choose or deliberately change their DNA — resistance arises from RANDOM mutations that existed before antibiotic exposure.
wrong_explanations.2: Antibiotics SELECT resistant variants — they don't cause the mutations. The mutations existed before the antibiotic was used.
wrong_explanations.3: Evolution occurs in all organisms — bacteria just evolve faster because they reproduce rapidly (millions of generations in days). Natural selection has been directly observed in larger organisms too.

higher.common_mistake: The fossil record is INCOMPLETE — most organisms never fossilise. This means gaps in the fossil record are EXPECTED and do not disprove evolution. Students often say 'if there are gaps in the fossil record, evolution can't be proven' — but the overall pattern of fossils strongly supports evolution, even with gaps.
higher.higher_box: Speciation provides the mechanism for the origin of new species through natural selection — it is the endpoint of divergent evolution from a common ancestor. Modern systematics uses DNA sequences and phylogenetic trees rather than physical appearance for classification. Convergent evolution (similar structures evolving independently in unrelated species due to similar selection pressures) must be distinguished from homologous structures (shared ancestry).

inheritance/fossils-extinction.summary: Describe how fossils form and explain why species become extinct.

quiz.q: Why are soft-bodied organisms rarely found as fossils?
opts: Soft tissue decays quickly — it usually decomposes before mineralisation can occur
Soft-bodied organisms don't have cells, so they cannot be fossilised
Soft-bodied organisms only live in water — fossils only form on land
Soft tissue is preserved perfectly but scientists cannot identify it

wrong_explanations.1: All living things have cells — soft-bodied organisms (jellyfish, worms etc.) are eukaryotes with normal cells.
wrong_explanations.2: Many fossils DO form in water and marine sediments — in fact, aquatic environments are often BETTER for fossilisation because rapid burial in sediment is more likely.
wrong_explanations.3: Soft tissue can be preserved in exceptional circumstances (amber, ice, peat bogs) — but in most cases it decays before preservation can occur.

quiz.q: The dodo became extinct in the 17th century. What caused its extinction?
opts: Human hunting and introduced species (rats and pigs eating eggs) — the dodo had no natural predators and was not afraid of humans
A catastrophic volcanic eruption on Mauritius wiped out the entire population
Climate change caused the fruit the dodo ate to disappear
A deadly virus spread through the population before any could survive and develop immunity

wrong_explanations.1: No major volcanic eruption caused the dodo's extinction — it was a combination of human hunting and the introduction of invasive species.
wrong_explanations.2: Some habitat change occurred, but the primary drivers were hunting and invasive species, not climate-driven food loss.
wrong_explanations.3: While disease can cause extinction, the primary documented causes for the dodo are human hunting and introduced species.

foundation.common_mistake: Extinction is PERMANENT — a species cannot come back once all individuals are dead. The fossil record being incomplete does NOT disprove evolution — it is expected and understandable given how rare fossilisation is. Most organisms that ever lived left no fossil at all.

inheritance/resistant-bacteria.summary: Explain how antibiotic resistance develops through natural selection and why it is a global health threat.

quiz.q: How does antibiotic resistance develop in bacteria?
opts: A random mutation gives some bacteria resistance — antibiotics kill non-resistant bacteria but resistant ones survive and reproduce
Bacteria deliberately mutate themselves when exposed to antibiotics to survive
Antibiotics cause the mutation that produces resistance in bacteria
Resistant bacteria migrate from other areas after non-resistant bacteria are killed

wrong_explanations.1: Bacteria cannot deliberately change their DNA — mutations are RANDOM and SPONTANEOUS. Bacteria cannot respond to threats by choosing to mutate.
wrong_explanations.2: Antibiotics are the SELECTION PRESSURE — they select pre-existing resistant variants. They do not cause the mutations that produce resistance.
wrong_explanations.3: Migration can spread resistance — but the PRIMARY mechanism of resistance developing in a population is natural selection of pre-existing resistant mutants.

quiz.q: Why is it important to complete a full course of antibiotics even if you feel better?
opts: Stopping early leaves the most resistant bacteria alive — they survive to reproduce and pass on resistance genes
The remaining antibiotics are needed to prevent the infection returning to full strength
Unfinished antibiotics are wasted medicine that could have been used by others
Stopping early makes the side effects of antibiotics worse

wrong_explanations.1: Infections can return if bacteria are not fully cleared — but the MORE IMPORTANT reason in terms of antibiotic resistance is that the survivors of partial treatment are the most resistant bacteria.
wrong_explanations.2: While waste is a concern, the primary medical reason is resistance selection — the survivors of an incomplete course are more likely to be resistant.
wrong_explanations.3: Side effects are not affected by course completion — the resistance argument is the primary public health reason.

quiz.q: MRSA is resistant to many antibiotics. How did this resistance arise?
opts: Through natural selection — repeated antibiotic use in hospitals selected for bacteria with resistance mutations, which then multiplied
MRSA was deliberately engineered in a laboratory as a biological weapon
MRSA infected humans from animals and naturally carries more mutations than other bacteria
Hospital cleaning products cause bacteria to mutate and become resistant

wrong_explanations.1: MRSA arose naturally through the evolutionary process of natural selection — not deliberate engineering.
wrong_explanations.2: Staphylococcus aureus is primarily a human pathogen — while zoonotic transmission does occur, MRSA's resistance arose through natural selection in hospital environments.
wrong_explanations.3: Cleaning products can select for resistance to disinfectants — but antibiotic resistance specifically arises from exposure to antibiotics, not cleaning agents.

foundation.common_mistake: Antibiotics do NOT CAUSE mutations — they SELECT resistant bacteria that already exist. The mutation happened randomly, long before the antibiotic was used. The antibiotic is the selection pressure that determines which bacteria survive. This is a key distinction — evolution acts on existing variation, it doesn't create new variation in response to need.

Students should be able to explain why resistance is evolutionarily inevitable if antibiotics are overused — resistant variants will always be selected for.

ecology/ecosystems.summary: Define ecosystem, population, community and habitat, and explain how organisms depend on each other.

quiz.q: What is the difference between a community and an ecosystem?
opts: A community is all the living organisms in an area. An ecosystem is the community PLUS all the non-living (abiotic) factors.
A community is larger than an ecosystem — it includes several ecosystems.
They are the same thing — both describe all organisms in an area.
An ecosystem only includes animals. A community includes all living things.

wrong_explanations.1: An ECOSYSTEM is larger — it includes the community PLUS all abiotic factors. A community is just the living part.
wrong_explanations.2: They are NOT the same — a community is the living component only. An ecosystem adds the non-living environment.
wrong_explanations.3: An ecosystem includes ALL living organisms (animals, plants, fungi, microorganisms) plus abiotic factors — not just animals.

quiz.q: Wolves are removed from a woodland ecosystem. What is the most likely consequence?
opts: Deer population increases — deer overgraze vegetation — plant diversity declines — other animals that depend on those plants also decline
The ecosystem immediately collapses — all species die without wolves
Other predators increase in number to compensate for the wolves
Nothing changes — deer populations regulate themselves

wrong_explanations.1: Ecosystems rarely collapse immediately — they often shift to a new, less diverse stable state. But the cascade of effects described is accurate.
wrong_explanations.2: Some compensation by other predators may occur — but wolves are often keystone predators, so full compensation is rare.
wrong_explanations.3: Without predators, prey populations often grow beyond what the habitat can support — leading to overgrazing and eventual population crash.

quiz.q: Why do artificial ecosystems (like farmland) tend to have lower biodiversity than natural ecosystems?
opts: They are dominated by one or a few crop species — most other species are excluded or removed
Artificial ecosystems are physically smaller than natural ones
Artificial ecosystems have less sunlight because they are sheltered
Animals avoid artificial ecosystems because they can detect human presence

wrong_explanations.1: Some farms are physically large — size is not the primary reason. MONOCULTURE (one crop species) and removal of 'weeds' and 'pests' dramatically reduces biodiversity.
wrong_explanations.2: Farmland typically receives as much sunlight as natural ecosystems — light is not the reason for lower biodiversity.
wrong_explanations.3: Many animals do live on farmland — but the monoculture crop structure and use of pesticides/herbicides removes habitat and food for most species.

higher.higher_box: Students should be able to explain how changes to abiotic and biotic factors affect communities, using data as evidence. Stable communities have balanced interactions — changes to one component ripple through the whole system. The concept of the ecosystem as a self-sustaining unit with inputs (sunlight, inorganic nutrients) and outputs (heat, waste) is important for understanding sustainability.

ecology/abiotic-biotic-factors.summary: Describe the abiotic and biotic factors that affect the distribution and abundance of organisms.

quiz.q: A river becomes polluted and oxygen levels fall. Which of the following is an abiotic factor that has changed?
opts: O₂ concentration — a non-living chemical factor that affects aquatic organisms
Fish population — the fish that die because of low oxygen
Disease — bacteria cause the oxygen to fall
Competition — fish compete for the remaining oxygen

wrong_explanations.1: Fish population = a BIOTIC factor — it is a living component. The O₂ level is the abiotic factor that changed.
wrong_explanations.2: Disease is a BIOTIC factor — caused by living pathogens. O₂ concentration is abiotic (a chemical feature of the water).
wrong_explanations.3: Competition is a BIOTIC factor between living organisms. The O₂ level itself is the abiotic factor.

quiz.q: Grey squirrels were introduced to the UK and have outcompeted red squirrels. What type of factor is this?
opts: Biotic — interspecific competition between two different species for food and habitat
Abiotic — grey squirrels changed the temperature of the habitat
Biotic — intraspecific competition between individuals of the same species
Abiotic — grey squirrels are larger so they change the physical environment

wrong_explanations.1: Grey squirrels don't change temperature — their effect is through direct competition for food and habitat, which is a biotic factor.
wrong_explanations.2: INTRASPECIFIC competition = same species. Red vs grey = TWO DIFFERENT SPECIES = INTERSPECIFIC competition.
wrong_explanations.3: Grey squirrels being larger is a physical trait — their impact on red squirrels is through competition for resources (a biotic factor).

foundation.common_mistake: Students often list only one or two abiotic factors and forget others. The full list includes: temperature, light intensity, water availability, soil pH, soil mineral content, wind speed, CO₂ concentration and O₂ concentration. In exams, be specific about which factor and exactly HOW it affects the organism.

higher.higher_box: Indicator species are used to assess environmental quality: lichens indicate clean air (absent where SO₂ levels are high); mayfly larvae indicate clean, well-oxygenated water; bloodworms tolerate moderate pollution; rat-tailed maggots tolerate severe pollution. Students should be able to interpret data about indicator species distributions and draw conclusions about environmental quality.

ecology/adaptations.summary: Describe structural, behavioural and functional adaptations and explain how they help survival.

quiz.q: A polar bear has thick white fur and a layer of blubber. What type of adaptations are these?
opts: Structural — physical features of the body that improve survival in cold environments
Behavioural — things the bear does to stay warm
Functional — internal processes that generate extra body heat
Environmental — the environment shaped the bear's appearance directly

wrong_explanations.1: Behavioural adaptations are things the organism DOES — e.g. hibernating or huddling. Fur and blubber are physical STRUCTURAL features.
wrong_explanations.2: Functional (physiological) adaptations are internal chemical processes — e.g. antifreeze proteins, concentrated urine production. Fur and blubber are physical structures.
wrong_explanations.3: The environment selects for adaptations through natural selection — but adaptations are features OF the organism, not caused directly by the environment.

quiz.q: Why do desert animals like the fennec fox have very large ears?
opts: Large ears increase the surface area for heat loss — helping the animal cool down in the hot desert
Large ears help the animal hear predators from further away in the flat desert
Large ears store water for use during droughts
Large ears are a warning signal to predators that the animal is dangerous

wrong_explanations.1: Hearing is enhanced too — but the PRIMARY ecological reason for the fennec fox's extraordinarily large ears relative to body size is thermoregulation (heat loss). Other desert animals with large ears (jackrabbits) also use them for cooling.
wrong_explanations.2: Ears don't store water — water is stored in fat (camels) or conserved through concentrated urine (kangaroo rats). The large ear surface area is for cooling.
wrong_explanations.3: Warning colouration (aposematism) typically involves bright colours — large plain ears in desert animals are not a warning signal.

foundation.common_mistake: Adaptations are NOT something an organism CHOOSES or deliberately develops. Organisms cannot decide to grow a thicker coat because it is cold — adaptations arise through natural selection over many generations. A single organism cannot adapt during its lifetime through genetic change.

ecology/food-chains-webs.summary: Describe food chains and webs, trophic levels and energy transfer between them.

quiz.q: Why do food chains rarely have more than 4–5 trophic levels?
opts: Energy is lost at each level — so little remains by the top level that it cannot support many organisms
Top predators are too large to find enough food in most ecosystems
Longer food chains would be too complex for organisms to navigate
Producers cannot support more than 4 levels of consumers

wrong_explanations.1: Size can increase up a food chain, but the limiting factor is ENERGY, not size. Even small top predators need large amounts of food.
wrong_explanations.2: Food chains are not 'navigated' by organisms — the limit is thermodynamic (energy loss), not cognitive.
wrong_explanations.3: Producers can support more levels in principle — the limit is the progressive loss of energy at each transfer, not some fixed rule about producers.

quiz.q: In a food web, the rabbit population suddenly increases due to a disease killing foxes. What happens to the grass?
opts: Grass decreases — more rabbits eat more grass
Grass increases — without foxes, more nutrients return to soil
Grass is unaffected — plants are not part of the animal food web
Grass increases — rabbits and grass have a mutualistic relationship

wrong_explanations.1: Fox death → reduced decomposition adds some nutrients, but the dominant effect is that MORE RABBITS eat MORE GRASS → grass DECREASES.
wrong_explanations.2: Plants ARE part of food webs — as producers. They are directly affected by herbivore (rabbit) population changes.
wrong_explanations.3: Rabbits eat grass — this is a consumer-producer relationship (predation/herbivory), not mutualism. Rabbits benefit; grass is harmed.

foundation.common_mistake: Arrows in food chains show the direction of ENERGY FLOW — from what is eaten to what eats it. So the arrow goes FROM prey TO predator. Students often draw arrows the wrong way, showing what the predator eats rather than which way energy flows. Also: PRODUCERS are PLANTS (photosynthetic) — not all plants in a food chain are 'prey', they are the PRODUCERS.

higher.higher_box: Pyramids of biomass show dry mass at each trophic level — always narrowing upward. Pyramids of number can be inverted (e.g. one oak tree → thousands of insects). Efficiency of energy transfer: efficiency (%) = (biomass transferred ÷ biomass available) × 100. Typically ~10% efficient. This means 90% of biomass is lost at each level as heat (respiration), undigested material (faeces), and uneaten portions.

ecology/population-competition.summary: Explain what limits population size and how competition shapes communities.

quiz.q: In a predator-prey graph, the predator population peaks AFTER the prey population peaks. Why?
opts: There is a time lag — it takes time for predators to reproduce in response to increased food availability
Predators eat the prey before they can reproduce, causing prey to peak first
Prey always outnumber predators — so prey peaks are automatically earlier
Predators migrate to different areas before their numbers can increase

wrong_explanations.1: The lag is specifically about REPRODUCTION — it takes time for increased food to result in increased offspring. Predators eating prey actually reduces prey numbers.
wrong_explanations.2: Prey outnumbering predators is normal — but the timing of peaks is about reproduction lag, not relative numbers.
wrong_explanations.3: Migration can occur — but the time lag in predator-prey cycles is primarily a biological response time, not migration.

quiz.q: Why is intraspecific competition more intense than interspecific competition?
opts: Individuals of the same species have identical needs — they compete for exactly the same resources
Individuals of the same species are physically stronger and dominate others
Intraspecific competition involves more individuals — so it is more intense by numbers
Different species never compete — they always occupy different niches

wrong_explanations.1: Physical strength varies within a species — the intensity of competition is about OVERLAP OF RESOURCE NEEDS, not strength.
wrong_explanations.2: Many species are common — but interspecific competition can also involve large numbers. The intensity difference is about the degree of resource overlap.
wrong_explanations.3: Different species CAN compete — grey and red squirrels are a clear UK example. The ecological niche concept says species that overlap too much cannot coexist indefinitely.

foundation.common_mistake: In predator-prey cycles, the PREDATOR population FOLLOWS the prey — with a time lag. Predator numbers rise AFTER prey numbers rise (because it takes time to breed). Predator numbers fall AFTER prey decline. Students often draw them rising and falling at the same time — they should be slightly out of phase.

quiz.q: In a predator-prey graph, the predator population peaks AFTER the prey population peaks. Why?
opts: There is a time lag — it takes time for predators to reproduce in response to increased food availability
Predators eat the prey before they can reproduce, causing prey to peak first
Prey always outnumber predators — so prey peaks are automatically earlier
Predators migrate to different areas before their numbers can increase

wrong_explanations.1: The lag is specifically about REPRODUCTION — it takes time for increased food to result in increased offspring. Predators eating prey actually reduces prey numbers.
wrong_explanations.2: Prey outnumbering predators is normal — but the timing of peaks is about reproduction lag, not relative numbers.
wrong_explanations.3: Migration can occur — but the time lag in predator-prey cycles is primarily a biological response time, not migration.

quiz.q: Why is intraspecific competition more intense than interspecific competition?
opts: Individuals of the same species have identical needs — they compete for exactly the same resources
Individuals of the same species are physically stronger and dominate others
Intraspecific competition involves more individuals — so it is more intense by numbers
Different species never compete — they always occupy different niches

wrong_explanations.1: Physical strength varies within a species — the intensity of competition is about OVERLAP OF RESOURCE NEEDS, not strength.
wrong_explanations.2: Many species are common — but interspecific competition can also involve large numbers. The intensity difference is about the degree of resource overlap.
wrong_explanations.3: Different species CAN compete — grey and red squirrels are a clear UK example. The ecological niche concept says species that overlap too much cannot coexist indefinitely.

higher.common_mistake: In predator-prey cycles, the PREDATOR population FOLLOWS the prey — with a time lag. Predator numbers rise AFTER prey numbers rise (because it takes time to breed). Predator numbers fall AFTER prey decline. Students often draw them rising and falling at the same time — they should be slightly out of phase.
higher.higher_box: Students should be able to interpret predator-prey population graphs — explaining the time lag between predator and prey peaks, and predicting the effects of changing one population on others in a food web. The carrying capacity is the maximum population that resources can sustain. Students should understand the concept of intraspecific competition as the primary density-dependent control on population size.

ecology/biodiversity.summary: Define biodiversity, explain its importance and describe threats to it and conservation measures.

quiz.q: Why is high genetic diversity within a species important?
opts: A genetically diverse population is more adaptable — some individuals will have alleles giving resistance to new diseases or tolerance of changing conditions
Genetic diversity makes individuals physically larger and stronger
Genetic diversity allows a species to reproduce more rapidly
Genetic diversity means all individuals look different — making them harder for predators to target

wrong_explanations.1: Genetic diversity doesn't directly affect physical size — size is just one trait among many.
wrong_explanations.2: Reproductive rate is not directly linked to genetic diversity — it depends on life history traits specific to the species.
wrong_explanations.3: Variation in appearance can help avoid predators — but the primary ecological importance of genetic diversity is ADAPTABILITY to change.

quiz.q: Why are seed banks important for biodiversity conservation?
opts: They store seeds of thousands of plant varieties — preserving genetic diversity as insurance against extinction of wild populations
They grow plants for replanting in habitats where species have become locally extinct
They store seeds of food crops only — to ensure human food security
They sell seeds to farmers to encourage growing a wider variety of crops

wrong_explanations.1: Seed banks primarily STORE seeds in long-term preservation conditions — they are not generally growing facilities or nurseries (though some seed banks do have growing programmes).
wrong_explanations.2: Seed banks like the Svalbard Global Seed Vault store seeds of wild plant species AND crop varieties — not food crops only.
wrong_explanations.3: Seed banks are conservation facilities — not commercial enterprises, though some seed collections are used to support farmers.

foundation.common_mistake: Biodiversity is NOT just about the NUMBER of species — it also includes the RELATIVE ABUNDANCE of each species. An ecosystem with 100 species but 99% of the biomass belonging to just one species has LOW biodiversity in practice. High biodiversity means many species AND reasonable numbers of each.

Students should be able to evaluate conservation strategies — protected areas, captive breeding, seed banks, habitat restoration — considering their effectiveness, cost and limitations. International agreements (e.g. Convention on Biological Diversity) coordinate global conservation efforts.

ecology/waste-management.summary: Explain how human population growth and rising living standards increase pollution of water, air and land, reducing biodiversity.

quiz.q: Why does a rising human standard of living increase pollution, even where population is stable?
opts: People use more resources and produce more waste per person
People automatically have more children
A higher standard of living has no effect on pollution
People move to the countryside where there is less waste

wrong_explanations.1: Standard of living is about resource use per person, not birth rate — both population size AND resource use per person raise pollution.
wrong_explanations.2: It is the extra resource use and waste per person that increases pollution, not the standard of living itself being harmless.
wrong_explanations.3: Where people go does not remove the waste — a higher standard of living means each person consumes and discards more.

quiz.q: Fertiliser washing into a river reduces biodiversity. Which environment is being polluted?
opts: Water
Air
Land only
None — fertiliser is not a pollutant

wrong_explanations.1: Fertiliser in a river is water pollution — it causes eutrophication, lowering oxygen and killing fish.
wrong_explanations.2: Fertiliser run-off enters water, not the air.
wrong_explanations.3: Fertiliser is a pollutant when it reaches water — it damages the ecosystem even though it helps crops grow on land.

foundation.common_mistake: Pollution is not only about litter you can see. Invisible pollution — sewage and fertiliser in water, acidic gases in air, pesticides soaking into land — often does the most damage to biodiversity. Also remember: MORE people AND a higher standard of living both increase waste, not just population size alone.

ecology/land-use.summary: Describe how humans reduce land available for other organisms, and explain why destroying peat bogs harms biodiversity and releases carbon dioxide.

quiz.q: Why does draining and extracting peat release carbon dioxide?
opts: Peat stores carbon from partly-decayed plants; decay or burning releases it as CO₂
Peat is made of pure carbon dioxide gas
Machines used to dig peat run on petrol
Peat reflects sunlight, warming the air

wrong_explanations.1: Peat is partly-decayed plant material that locked away carbon; when it decays in air or is burned, that carbon is released as CO₂.
wrong_explanations.2: Peat is solid plant material, not CO₂ gas — the CO₂ is released only when the stored carbon reacts with oxygen.
wrong_explanations.3: The machinery is a minor factor; the main release is the carbon stored in the peat itself escaping as CO₂.

quiz.q: Which single change would best protect peat bog biodiversity?
opts: Buying peat-free compost for gardens
Extracting peat faster so it is used up sooner
Burning peat instead of using it as compost
Draining more bogs to create farmland

wrong_explanations.1: If gardeners choose peat-free compost, demand for peat falls, so fewer bogs are destroyed and their biodiversity is protected.
wrong_explanations.2: Extracting peat faster destroys the habitat sooner — the opposite of protecting it.
wrong_explanations.3: Burning peat still destroys the bog and releases CO₂.

foundation.common_mistake: Destroying a peat bog harms the environment in TWO ways, not one: it reduces biodiversity (habitat lost) AND releases carbon dioxide (stored carbon escapes as the peat decays or is burned). Many students only mention one. Peat is extracted mainly for COMPOST, not just as a fuel.

ecology/deforestation.summary: Explain the reasons for large-scale deforestation in tropical areas and its consequences for carbon dioxide levels and biodiversity.

quiz.q: How does deforestation lead to LESS carbon dioxide being removed from the atmosphere?
opts: Fewer trees means less photosynthesis, so less CO₂ is absorbed
Trees release oxygen that destroys carbon dioxide
Cutting trees cools the planet, trapping CO₂ in the soil
Deforestation increases the number of decomposers

wrong_explanations.1: Trees remove CO₂ by photosynthesis; with fewer trees, less CO₂ is taken out of the air.
wrong_explanations.2: Photosynthesis absorbs CO₂ — oxygen release does not "destroy" carbon dioxide.
wrong_explanations.3: Removing trees reduces CO₂ uptake; it does not trap carbon in the soil.

quiz.q: Burning felled trees and the decay of leftover wood both release carbon dioxide. Why does decay release CO₂?
opts: Microorganisms respire as they break down the dead wood, releasing CO₂
Wood turns directly into carbon dioxide with no organisms involved
Decay absorbs carbon dioxide from the air
Only burning releases CO₂ — decay does not

wrong_explanations.1: Decomposer microorganisms respire while feeding on the dead wood, and respiration releases carbon dioxide.
wrong_explanations.2: Decay is a biological process — microorganisms carry it out; wood does not spontaneously become CO₂.
wrong_explanations.3: Decay releases CO₂, it does not absorb it.

foundation.common_mistake: Deforestation raises atmospheric CO₂ for TWO separate reasons: fewer trees means less CO₂ is absorbed by photosynthesis, AND burning or decay of the wood releases stored CO₂. Give both. Also, decay releases CO₂ because MICROORGANISMS respire as they break the wood down — it is a biological process.

ecology/global-warming.summary: Describe how rising carbon dioxide and methane cause global warming, and explain its biological consequences for species distribution, migration and biodiversity.

quiz.q: Global warming changes the "distribution" of a species. What does distribution mean?
opts: The geographic area where the species is found
The total number of individuals in the species
How the food is shared within a population
The genetic variety within the species

wrong_explanations.1: Distribution is the range — the places on Earth where a species lives. Warming shifts this range.
wrong_explanations.2: That is population size, not distribution.
wrong_explanations.3: That is not what distribution means in ecology.

quiz.q: Which pair of processes is causing sea levels to rise?
opts: Melting ice and thermal expansion of warmer water
Photosynthesis and respiration
More rain falling on land
Fish releasing carbon dioxide

wrong_explanations.1: Warming melts land ice (adding water) and makes existing sea water expand as it warms — both raise sea level.
wrong_explanations.2: Photosynthesis and respiration cycle carbon; they do not raise sea level.
wrong_explanations.3: Rainfall does not cause the long-term rise; melting ice and thermal expansion do.

foundation.common_mistake: In BIOLOGY, focus on the LIVING consequences of global warming — changes in species distribution, migration, and biodiversity — not just the greenhouse-gas chemistry. Also, 'distribution' means WHERE a species lives (its range), not how many there are. Rising sea level is caused by BOTH melting ice AND warm water expanding.

The genetic variety within the species

ecology/maintaining-biodiversity.summary: Describe the programmes humans use to reduce their impact on ecosystems and maintain biodiversity, and the conflicts these can involve.

quiz.q: Why might reintroducing hedgerows and field margins on a large single-crop farm increase biodiversity?
opts: They provide food and shelter for a wider range of species
They increase the yield of the single crop
They stop the farmer using any pesticides
They remove all competition between species

wrong_explanations.1: Hedgerows and margins add habitat variety, giving insects, birds and mammals food and shelter, so more species can live there.
wrong_explanations.2: Their main benefit here is biodiversity, not crop yield.
wrong_explanations.3: Hedgerows help wildlife but do not automatically stop pesticide use.

quiz.q: Conservation programmes often cause conflict. Which is an example of that conflict?
opts: Protecting a habitat reduces land available for farming or building
Protecting a habitat always makes everyone wealthier
Conservation never costs any money
Endangered species breed better without any human help

wrong_explanations.1: Land set aside for conservation cannot be used for farms or houses — that is the trade-off with human needs.
wrong_explanations.2: Conservation has costs and can limit development, so it does not always make everyone wealthier.
wrong_explanations.3: Conservation programmes need funding — that cost is exactly what creates conflict.

foundation.common_mistake: 'Maintaining biodiversity' is about active human PROGRAMMES (breeding, protecting habitats, hedgerows, recycling, cutting emissions), not just 'stop polluting'. Also be ready to explain the CONFLICT: conservation costs money and land, so it competes with farming, building and jobs.

ecology/carbon-cycle.summary: Describe how carbon is cycled through ecosystems and the atmosphere.

quiz.q: Why does burning fossil fuels increase atmospheric CO₂ more than burning wood?
opts: Fossil fuels contain carbon that has been stored underground for millions of years — releasing it rapidly adds ancient carbon that had been removed from the active cycle
Fossil fuels burn hotter, so they release more CO₂ per reaction
Wood absorbs CO₂ as it burns, partially cancelling its emissions
Fossil fuels contain more hydrogen, which converts to CO₂ during combustion

wrong_explanations.1: Combustion temperature doesn't determine CO₂ output — the amount of carbon in the fuel does.
wrong_explanations.2: Wood does not absorb CO₂ while burning — it releases it. The difference is that wood carbon was recently absorbed from the atmosphere, while fossil fuel carbon was locked away for millions of years.
wrong_explanations.3: Hydrogen in fuels combines with oxygen to form WATER, not CO₂. Carbon combines with oxygen to form CO₂.

quiz.q: How does deforestation affect atmospheric CO₂ levels?
opts: Removes photosynthesising trees (less CO₂ absorbed) AND burning or rotting felled trees releases CO₂ — a double increase in atmospheric CO₂
Deforestation only affects CO₂ if the wood is burned — leaving it to rot has no effect
Deforestation decreases CO₂ because the dead wood is converted to fossil fuels underground
Deforestation has no effect on CO₂ — other plants regrow in the cleared areas quickly

wrong_explanations.1: Rotting (decomposition) DOES release CO₂ — decomposers respire and return carbon to the atmosphere, just more slowly than burning.
wrong_explanations.2: Fossil fuel formation takes millions of years under specific geological conditions — dead wood does not become fossil fuel on human timescales.
wrong_explanations.3: Regrowth does occur — but typically much less vegetation regrows than was removed, and regeneration takes decades. The net effect is increased atmospheric CO₂.

foundation.common_mistake: PLANTS also respire — they release CO₂ just like animals. The difference is that during the DAY, photosynthesis removes MORE CO₂ than respiration releases — so there is net CO₂ uptake. At night, only respiration occurs. Students often say 'plants absorb CO₂, they don't release it' — this is wrong.

higher.higher_box: Human impacts on the carbon cycle: burning fossil fuels releases ancient sequestered carbon rapidly; deforestation removes carbon sinks AND releases stored carbon. Rising atmospheric CO₂ enhances the greenhouse effect causing global warming and climate change. Ocean acidification — CO₂ dissolves in seawater forming carbonic acid — threatens coral reefs and shell-forming organisms. Students should understand the distinction between the short-term carbon cycle and the long-term geological cycle.

ecology/water-cycle.summary: Describe how water is cycled through the environment.

quiz.q: How do plants contribute to the water cycle?
opts: Through transpiration — water absorbed from soil is released as water vapour through leaf stomata, returning water to the atmosphere
By storing water permanently in their cells — removing it from the cycle
By producing water as a by-product of photosynthesis
By preventing precipitation by blocking clouds with their canopy

wrong_explanations.1: Plants don't store water permanently — they continuously take it in and release it. Transpiration is the return pathway to the atmosphere.
wrong_explanations.2: Photosynthesis USES water as a reactant — it does not produce water as a by-product. Oxygen is the gas product of photosynthesis.
wrong_explanations.3: Forests influence local weather patterns but do not prevent precipitation — they actually INCREASE local rainfall by contributing to atmospheric water vapour.

quiz.q: Why does deforestation in tropical areas often lead to reduced local rainfall?
opts: Trees release large amounts of water vapour through transpiration — removing them reduces the water returning to the atmosphere, lowering precipitation
Trees attract rain clouds — without trees, clouds move away from the area
Tree roots release chemicals that trigger condensation in the atmosphere above
Deforestation increases runoff — water leaves the area before it can evaporate

wrong_explanations.1: Clouds are not 'attracted' to trees — the mechanism is through transpiration contributing to atmospheric moisture content.
wrong_explanations.2: Roots do not release chemicals that trigger condensation — their role in the water cycle is absorbing water from soil, which then passes up to leaves and out through transpiration.
wrong_explanations.3: Increased runoff does remove water quickly — but the PRIMARY reason for reduced local rainfall after deforestation is the loss of transpiration returning water to the atmosphere.

foundation.common_mistake: TRANSPIRATION is the loss of water vapour from PLANT LEAVES through stomata — it is NOT the same as evaporation from the soil or water surface. Both contribute to atmospheric water, but transpiration is specifically the biological process in plants. Also: precipitation is NOT just rain — it includes snow, sleet and hail.

ecology/decomposition.summary: Describe the role of decomposers in nutrient cycling and the factors that affect decomposition rate.

quiz.q: What is the difference between decomposers and detritivores?
opts: Decomposers (bacteria, fungi) chemically break down organic matter using enzymes. Detritivores (earthworms, woodlice) physically shred it into smaller pieces.
Decomposers are larger organisms like earthworms. Detritivores are microscopic bacteria.
They are the same thing — both terms describe organisms that break down dead material.
Decomposers work in soil only. Detritivores work above ground only.

wrong_explanations.1: DECOMPOSERS are the MICROSCOPIC organisms (bacteria and fungi). DETRITIVORES are LARGER invertebrates like earthworms, woodlice and millipedes.
wrong_explanations.2: They are NOT the same — the distinction is between CHEMICAL breakdown (decomposers) and PHYSICAL breakdown (detritivores). They complement each other.
wrong_explanations.3: Both decomposers and detritivores can work in soil or in leaf litter — the distinction is not about location but about HOW they break down material.

quiz.q: Why does food last longer in a refrigerator than at room temperature?
opts: Low temperature slows enzyme activity in decomposer bacteria and fungi — decomposition rate decreases significantly
The refrigerator removes oxygen from around the food — decomposers suffocate
The cold temperature kills all bacteria in the food immediately
Refrigerators produce chemicals that inhibit decomposer activity

wrong_explanations.1: Refrigerators don't remove oxygen — they simply cool the air. The reduced decomposition rate is due to lower enzyme activity at low temperatures.
wrong_explanations.2: Refrigeration SLOWS bacteria but doesn't kill them instantly — food still eventually spoils in the fridge. Freezing is needed to effectively stop bacteria.
wrong_explanations.3: Refrigerators only cool — they don't produce inhibitory chemicals.

foundation.common_mistake: DECOMPOSERS are bacteria and fungi — they chemically break down organic matter using enzymes. DETRITIVORES (earthworms, woodlice) physically shred material into smaller pieces — increasing surface area for decomposers. Detritivores do NOT chemically decompose material. Both are important but they are different things.

quiz.q: What is the difference between decomposers and detritivores?

Decomposers are larger organisms like earthworms. Detritivores are microscopic bacteria.
They are the same thing — both terms describe organisms that break down dead material.
Decomposers work in soil only. Detritivores work above ground only.

higher.higher_box: Rate of decomposition depends on: temperature (enzyme activity), moisture (decomposers need water), O₂ availability (aerobic decomposers work faster), pH (optimum pH for decomposer enzymes). Compost heaps are managed to optimise these conditions. Biogas digesters use anaerobic bacteria to produce methane from organic waste — used as a renewable fuel. Students should be able to design an experiment to investigate the effect of one factor on decomposition rate.

ecology/sampling-techniques.summary: Describe how to estimate population size using quadrats, transects and mark-recapture.

quiz.q: A student places 10 quadrats (each 1 m²) randomly in a field (total area 500 m²). Mean dandelion count = 4 per quadrat. Estimate the dandelion population.
opts: 2000 — mean count (4) × (total area ÷ quadrat area) = 4 × 500 = 2000
40 — mean count × number of quadrats = 4 × 10
500 — total area of field
50 — total area ÷ number of quadrats

wrong_explanations.1: 40 gives the total counted in ALL quadrats — not the estimated population of the whole field.
wrong_explanations.2: 500 is just the field area — it has nothing to do with the dandelion count.
wrong_explanations.3: 50 is just a spatial measurement — the estimated population requires scaling the mean count up to the full habitat area.

quiz.q: In a mark-recapture study: 25 snails marked (n₁), 20 caught second time (n₂), 5 were marked (m). What is the estimated population?
opts: 250 — N = 25 × 20 ÷ 2 (dividing by wrong number)
2500 — N = 25 × 20 × 5 (multiplying instead of dividing)

wrong_explanations.1: N = (n₁ × n₂) ÷ m = (25 × 20) ÷ 5 = 500 ÷ 5 = 100. Make sure to divide by m (number of marked in second catch = 5), not by n₂.
wrong_explanations.2: N = (n₁ × n₂) ÷ m = (25 × 20) ÷ 5 = 100. Multiplying gives an enormous overestimate.
wrong_explanations.3: The formula is N = (n₁ × n₂) ÷ m — not the inverse. Dividing m by the product gives a nonsensically small number.

quiz.q: Why must quadrats be placed RANDOMLY in a habitat?
opts: To avoid sampling bias — deliberately choosing areas with lots of organisms would overestimate the population
Random placement ensures all quadrats are the same size
Random placement prevents disturbing the organisms in the habitat
It is a legal requirement for ecological surveys

wrong_explanations.1: Quadrat size is fixed by the frame — it doesn't change with placement method.
wrong_explanations.2: Disturbance is minimised by careful technique — but the reason for random placement is specifically about statistical validity and avoiding bias.
wrong_explanations.3: There is no legal requirement for randomness — it is a scientific requirement to ensure the sample is representative of the whole habitat.

foundation.common_mistake: In the mark-recapture formula N = (n₁ × n₂) ÷ m — students often confuse what n₁, n₂ and m represent. n₁ = first catch (all marked). n₂ = second catch (total number caught). m = marked individuals IN the second catch. Do NOT divide by n₂ or mix up m and n₂.

fifas.question: A student catches 40 woodlice, marks them and releases them. The next day, they catch 30 woodlice and find that 6 are marked. Estimate the population.

higher.higher_box: Mark-recapture formula: N = (n₁ × n₂) ÷ m. Assumptions: random mixing of marked animals, no significant births/deaths/migration between captures, marking does not affect survival or recapture probability. Students should be able to evaluate the validity of these assumptions in given scenarios. Students should be able to use random number tables or coordinates to ensure random quadrat placement, reducing sampling bias.
